90	<rule>Unit tests passing alone is NOT sufficient to mark a criterion as "pass" — the verification method in the contract must be executed</rule>
91	<rule>If the contract criterion says "Run: <command>" you MUST run that exact command and record the result</rule>
92	</rules>
93	
94	<quality-checklist>
95	<check category="Bugs">None-checks, fehlende Parameter-Validierung, falsche Typen</check>
96	<check category="API-Kompatibilitaet">Werden bestehende Funktionssignaturen gebrochen?</check>
97	<check category="Unvollstaendige-Arbeit">TODOs, auskommentierter Code, fehlende Imports</check>
98	<check category="Logik-Fehler">Off-by-one, falsche Vergleiche, fehlende Edge Cases</check>
99	<check category="E2e-Verifikation">ALLE verification_commands aus test-harness.json ausfuehren — Ergebnisse als evidence aufnehmen</check>
100	</quality-checklist>
101	
102	<constraints>
103	<constraint>Du bist NUR Reviewer. Aendere KEINEN Code.</constraint>
104	<constraint>Output ONLY the JSON file — no markdown, no commentary</constraint>
105	<constraint>The file path MUST be ${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/reviews/phase-{PHASE_ID}.json</constraint>
106	<constraint>Do NOT spawn sub-agents or call the Agent tool. You are a single-pass reviewer — write the review file, validate it, and stop. Do NOT launch another reviewer for "iteration 2" or any other reason.</constraint>
107	<constraint>Infrastructure failures (service down, missing env vars, connection refused) → criterion verdict "warn" (not "fail") and INFO-level findings (not CRITICAL). Use "fail" only for genuine code defects where the code was executed and produced wrong results.</constraint>
108	<constraint>A "pass" verdict without evidence for behavioral/boundary criteria is INVALID — the orchestrator will reject it. Structural criteria may pass based on build/lint results alone.</constraint>
109	<constraint>Verify, don't vibe — you MUST execute verification commands literally and record actual output as evidence. No pattern-matching, no "looks right to me." See `hi/playbook/canon/verify-dont-vibe.md`.</constraint>
110	<constraint>No sycophancy — if the executor's work is incorrect, say so with specific findings. Do not mark criteria pass to avoid conflict. See `hi/playbook/canon/no-sycophancy-rule.md`.</constraint>
111	<constraint>Scope discipline — flag unexpected scope expansion in the executor's diff as a finding. Changes outside the task's declared files_to_modify are suspect. See `hi/playbook/canon/scope-is-sacred.md`.</constraint>
112	<constraint>Scratch state hygiene — flag any committed files under `.dirigent/`, `.dirigent-onboarding/`, `.planning/`, or other scratch dirs as a critical finding. See `hi/playbook/canon/scratch-state-hygiene.md`.</constraint>
113	</constraints>
114	
115	## Validation (MANDATORY)
116	
117	After writing the review JSON, validate it:
118	
119	```bash
120	python ${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}/scripts/validate_schema.py ${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/reviews/phase-{PHASE_ID}.json
121	```
122	
123	If validation fails, fix the errors and re-run until it passes.
124

file.content:
---
name: review-phase
description: Review code changes from a completed phase against the contract (reviewer role)
context: fork
agent: reviewer
---

<role>Du bist der REVIEWER. Du pruefst Aenderungen gegen den Contract und gibst ein PASS/FAIL Verdict. Du aenderst KEINEN Code.</role>

<instructions>
<step id="1">Read the contract from `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/contracts/phase-{PHASE_ID}.json` to understand the acceptance criteria.</step>
<step id="1b">If `.brv/context-tree/` exists and `brv` CLI is available, run `brv query "What domain rules and patterns apply to phase {PHASE_ID}?"` to understand domain expectations the code should meet.</step>
<step id="1c">Detect if this is the final phase: count all phase IDs in `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/PLAN.json`. If this phase's ID is the highest, it is the **final phase** — stricter e2e requirements apply (see rules below).</step>
<step id="1d">Read `./ARCHITECTURE.md` from the repo root (if it exists) to understand the configured e2e framework, test infrastructure, and dev-server setup.</step>
<step id="2">Run `git diff HEAD~{COMMITS}` to see all changes made during this phase. Examine each changed file.</step>
<step id="3">For each acceptance criterion, EXECUTE the verification method described in the criterion. Do NOT judge pass/fail by reading code alone — you MUST run the actual command and record the output as evidence.</step>
<step id="4">Check for code quality issues: bugs, broken API compatibility, incomplete work, logic errors.</step>
<step id="5">If `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/test-harness.json` exists, run the MANDATORY e2e verification steps described below. This is NOT optional.</step>
<step id="6">Determine the overall verdict using the strict rules below.</step>
<step id="7">Write the review JSON to `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/reviews/phase-{PHASE_ID}.json` using the exact schema below.</step>
</instructions>

<e2e-verification hint="MANDATORY when ${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/test-harness.json exists">
<step id="5a">Read `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/test-harness.json` to get the test harness.</step>
<step id="5b">Run each health_check command to confirm the environment is alive. If a health check fails, note it as a finding but do not FAIL the review for infrastructure issues.</step>
<step id="5c">If auth.login_command is set, run it to obtain a token/session. Use it for subsequent requests.</step>
<step id="5d">Run each verification_command. Check if the result matches the "expected" field. If a verification command fails and it relates to an acceptance criterion, mark that criterion as "fail" and include the command output in evidence.</step>
<step id="5e">If e2e_framework.run_command is set and the framework is configured, run the e2e test suite. Report failures as findings with severity "critical".</step>
<step id="5f">**Final phase enforcement:** If this is the final phase AND no criterion in the contract uses an e2e framework command (playwright/cypress/detox/pytest --e2e), OR all such criteria lack evidence in your results — add a critical finding: `"Final phase requires e2e verification evidence — none found."` and set verdict to "fail".</step>
</e2e-verification>

<output file="${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/reviews/phase-{PHASE_ID}.json">
{
  "phase_id": "01",
  "iteration": 1,
  "verdict": "pass or fail",
  "criteria_results": [
    {
      "ac_id": "AC-01-01",
      "verdict": "pass",
      "notes": "Criterion met because X",
      "evidence": [
        {
          "command": "curl -sf http://localhost:3000/api/health",
          "exit_code": 0,
          "stdout_snippet": "{\"status\":\"ok\"}",
          "stderr_snippet": ""
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "ac_id": "AC-01-02",
      "verdict": "fail",
      "notes": "Not met: expected Y but found Z",
      "evidence": [
        {
          "command": "npx playwright test --grep 'feature'",
          "exit_code": 1,
          "stdout_snippet": "1 failed\n  Error: expected 'Dashboard' but got 'Login'",
          "stderr_snippet": ""
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "findings": [
    {
      "severity": "critical",
      "file": "src/foo.py",
      "line": 42,
      "description": "Null check missing — will crash when input is None",
      "suggestion": "Add 'if x is None: return' guard"
    }
  ],
  "summary": "Overall assessment of the phase"
}
</output>

<rules>
<rule>The "verdict" field MUST be exactly "pass" or "fail" (lowercase)</rule>
<rule>Verdict is "fail" if ANY criteria_results entry has verdict "fail"</rule>
<rule>Verdict is "fail" if ANY finding has severity "critical"</rule>
<rule>Verdict is "fail" if ANY behavioral or boundary criterion has verdict "pass" but EMPTY evidence — you cannot declare pass without proof</rule>
<rule>Every criteria_results entry MUST reference an ac_id from the contract</rule>
<rule>Every behavioral/boundary criteria_results entry MUST include at least one evidence entry with the actual command run and its output — structural criteria may pass without evidence</rule>
<rule>Every finding MUST reference a specific file and line number</rule>
<rule>The "iteration" field must match the --iteration argument</rule>
<rule>The output MUST be valid JSON matching the schema exactly</rule>
<rule>If e2e verification commands fail, include the command output in the finding notes AND in the criterion evidence</rule>
<rule>If this is the final phase, at least one criteria_results entry MUST contain evidence from an e2e framework run (playwright/cypress/detox/pytest --e2e). Missing e2e evidence on a final phase = automatic "fail".</rule>
<rule>Unit tests passing alone is NOT sufficient to mark a criterion as "pass" — the verification method in the contract must be executed</rule>
<rule>If the contract criterion says "Run: <command>" you MUST run that exact command and record the result</rule>
</rules>

<quality-checklist>
<check category="Bugs">None-checks, fehlende Parameter-Validierung, falsche Typen</check>
<check category="API-Kompatibilitaet">Werden bestehende Funktionssignaturen gebrochen?</check>
<check category="Unvollstaendige-Arbeit">TODOs, auskommentierter Code, fehlende Imports</check>
<check category="Logik-Fehler">Off-by-one, falsche Vergleiche, fehlende Edge Cases</check>
<check category="E2e-Verifikation">ALLE verification_commands aus test-harness.json ausfuehren — Ergebnisse als evidence aufnehmen</check>
</quality-checklist>

<constraints>
<constraint>Du bist NUR Reviewer. Aendere KEINEN Code.</constraint>
<constraint>Output ONLY the JSON file — no markdown, no commentary</constraint>
<constraint>The file path MUST be ${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/reviews/phase-{PHASE_ID}.json</constraint>
<constraint>Do NOT spawn sub-agents or call the Agent tool. You are a single-pass reviewer — write the review file, validate it, and stop. Do NOT launch another reviewer for "iteration 2" or any other reason.</constraint>
<constraint>Infrastructure failures (service down, missing env vars, connection refused) → criterion verdict "warn" (not "fail") and INFO-level findings (not CRITICAL). Use "fail" only for genuine code defects where the code was executed and produced wrong results.</constraint>
<constraint>A "pass" verdict without evidence for behavioral/boundary criteria is INVALID — the orchestrator will reject it. Structural criteria may pass based on build/lint results alone.</constraint>
<constraint>Verify, don't vibe — you MUST execute verification commands literally and record actual output as evidence. No pattern-matching, no "looks right to me." See `hi/playbook/canon/verify-dont-vibe.md`.</constraint>
<constraint>No sycophancy — if the executor's work is incorrect, say so with specific findings. Do not mark criteria pass to avoid conflict. See `hi/playbook/canon/no-sycophancy-rule.md`.</constraint>
<constraint>Scope discipline — flag unexpected scope expansion in the executor's diff as a finding. Changes outside the task's declared files_to_modify are suspect. See `hi/playbook/canon/scope-is-sacred.md`.</constraint>
<constraint>Scratch state hygiene — flag any committed files under `.dirigent/`, `.dirigent-onboarding/`, `.planning/`, or other scratch dirs as a critical finding. See `hi/playbook/canon/scratch-state-hygiene.md`.</constraint>
</constraints>

## Validation (MANDATORY)

After writing the review JSON, validate it:

```bash
python ${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}/scripts/validate_schema.py ${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/reviews/phase-{PHASE_ID}.json
```

If validation fails, fix the errors and re-run until it passes.

content.text:
Memory saved. I re-read `create-contract/SKILL.md` with the UX lens, plus `review-phase/SKILL.md` because it's the downstream consumer I don't want to break. Here's what I found and what I propose.

## What's wrong in the current skill (concrete)

1. **Schema-first framing.** The file opens with "⛔ MANDATORY JSON SCHEMA" (line 12). The agent's first cognitive load is schema compliance, not user-outcome thinking. The "Would a user notice?" litmus test is rule #10 of 13 — buried.

2. **HTTP/API vocabulary baked into the layer definitions.** Layer 2 (behavioral) is taught with curl + jq examples. Layer 3 (boundary) is taught as HTTP 401/403/404 checks. The agent learns "behavioral = server returns right bytes" — which is exactly the drift you want killed.

3. **`objective` field drifts engineering-speak.** The worked example: *"Implement CRUD endpoints for user management with role-based access"*. A user doesn't want CRUD endpoints; they want to add/edit/remove users.

4. **Every example criterion is API-shaped.** None describe what the admin *sees on screen* when they add a user. `AC-01-02` through `AC-01-06` are all `curl | jq` tests about response bodies and status codes.

5. **`Run: <shell>` is mandatory for every verification** (line 37). This format forces user-facing checks into shell shape — which pushes agents toward curl (easy to write) and away from Playwright (harder but actually proves user impact).

6. **E2E-requirement is backloaded** — only the final phase needs a real user-flow test. Earlier phases can pass with "API works" and never prove anyone sees anything.

## What the reviewer depends on (the coupling I have to respect)

`review-phase/SKILL.md` literally executes `Run: <command>` and records stdout as evidence. It requires evidence for every behavioral/boundary criterion. **The `Run: <shell>` envelope must stay** — if I change the format, I break the reviewer and cascade into `fix_review`, portal evidence reporting, and the validator script. So the reframe sits *inside* the existing envelope: change what criteria *say* and *prove*, not the transport.

## The reframe I propose

**A. Invert the file order.** User-outcome lens up top, schema at the bottom as a validator. The agent reads "write criteria from the user's viewpoint" *before* it reads the JSON schema.

**B. Rename the layers to force the framing.**
- `structural` → kept (compile/lint)
- `behavioral` → **`user-journey`** (user does X, sees Y)
- `boundary` → **`edge-case`** (user does bad X, sees helpful Y)

Naming is cheap and does most of the work. An agent asked to write `user-journey` criteria will struggle to produce curl checks; the label fights the drift.

**C. Rewrite every worked example from the user POV.** Pairs to replace what's there now:

| Old (API-shaped) | New (user-shaped) |
|---|---|
| "GET /api/users returns a list with id/email/role" | "After logging in as admin and opening User Management, the admin sees existing users listed with their email and role" |
| "POST /api/users returns 201 with generated ID" | "The admin clicks 'Add User', fills email + role, submits, and sees the new row appear in the table without a page reload" |
| "POST with duplicate email returns 409" | "When the admin submits a form with an email already taken, they see 'This email is already registered' next to the email field and the form does not close" |
| "Viewer role cannot access admin endpoints — returns 403" | "A viewer does not see the 'User Management' link in the sidebar; opening /admin/users directly shows a 'You don't have access' message" |

**D. Default the verification engine to Playwright for user-visible criteria.** You already have `mcp__plugin_playwright_playwright__*` loaded. The skill should suggest Playwright first and curl only for pure-backend features (cron jobs, migrations, webhooks). The `Run:` shell command wrapping either becomes `Run: npx playwright test --grep "..."` — reviewer compat preserved.

**E. Add banned patterns that target THIS drift specifically.** Today's banned table targets `grep src/ for function name` (code-structure lies). Add:

| Pattern | Why it's bad | What to use instead |
|---|---|---|
| `curl ... \| grep '"status":"ok"'` as behavioral | Proves server replied, not that user saw anything | Playwright: navigate + assert visible text |
| `jq '.id'` as the only check for "feature works" | Response shape ≠ user experience | Playwright: create via UI, assert row renders |
| `test "$HTTP_CODE" = "403"` as user-facing boundary | Status code isn't what the user sees | Playwright: navigate + assert "access denied" UI |

**F. Require a cross-reference to the SPEC's user-outcome.** Each criterion description must begin with or echo a user-outcome from `SPEC.md`. If SPEC has no user-outcome section (most today won't), the contract skill synthesizes one from the Goal/Requirements and writes it back to SPEC so the next phase inherits it. This makes the contract skill the forcing function — we don't need to change `generate-spec` in lockstep.

**G. `objective` must be user-outcome phrasing.** Add a rule: "Starts with a verb the user performs, not the system performs." `"Admins can manage users — create, edit, disable"`, not `"Implement CRUD endpoints"`.

## What I deliberately leave alone

- Pydantic schema (field names, `AC-{PHASE}-{NN}`, min/max counts) — keeps reviewer/validator happy.
- `Run: <shell>` envelope — same.
- `expected_files` — reviewer uses it for scope-check.
- The downstream `review-phase` skill — doesn't need to change to consume user-shaped criteria.

## One real risk to name

The Playwright default assumes the target repo has a working Playwright setup + dev server. For repos where it doesn't, you'll get criteria that can't be verified until infra is in place. Mitigation: the skill should read `test-harness.json` and fall back to curl-shaped criteria *only* when `e2e_framework` is `"none"` — and flag that as a warning the reviewer sees.

## What do you want next?

Three options, not mutually exclusive:
1. I write the full reframed `create-contract/SKILL.md` for your review before committing.
2. I first draft just the top section + the new layer names + worked examples, so you can eyeball the framing before I touch the 280 lines of schema/rules.
3. Push back on any of the specific proposals above (especially B/D/F — those are the ones with the highest blast radius).

Which?

"""
Contract & Review schemas — typed representations for phase contracts and reviews.

Contracts define acceptance criteria before a phase starts.
Reviews evaluate changes against those criteria after execution.
Both are JSON files validated by Pydantic so Python never string-matches markdown.
"""

import json
from enum import Enum
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Optional

from loguru import logger
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field


# ══════════════════════════════════════════
# ENUMS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════

class CriterionLayer(str, Enum):
    STRUCTURAL = "structural"   # File exists, compiles, router registered
    BEHAVIORAL = "behavioral"   # Feature works: HTTP calls, auth, data persistence
    BOUNDARY = "boundary"       # Edge cases: error paths, malformed input, duplicates


class CriterionVerdict(str, Enum):
    PASS = "pass"
    FAIL = "fail"
    WARN = "warn"


class Verdict(str, Enum):
    PASS = "pass"
    FAIL = "fail"


class FindingSeverity(str, Enum):
    CRITICAL = "critical"
    WARN = "warn"
    INFO = "info"


# ══════════════════════════════════════════
# CONTRACT SCHEMA
# ══════════════════════════════════════════

class AcceptanceCriterion(BaseModel):
    """A single measurable acceptance criterion."""
    id: str = Field(..., description="Unique ID like AC-01-01")
    description: str = Field(..., description="What must be true")
    verification: str = Field(..., description="Executable command to verify (Run: ...)")
    layer: CriterionLayer = CriterionLayer.BEHAVIORAL


class ExpectedFileChange(BaseModel):
    """A file expected to change during the phase."""
    path: str
    change: str = Field(..., description="What changes in this file")

content.text:
Before drafting, one schema reality: `CriterionLayer` is a closed enum with `structural | behavioral | boundary`. A rename to `user-journey` / `edge-case` is a 3-line change in `contract_schema.py` — small, but it touches the Python side. I'll draft using the new names; the enum rename ships with the skill change or we keep the old stored values and translate. Flagging now, deciding at the end.

## The greenfield fix: phase-kind classification

The contract skill can't uniformly demand user-journey criteria. In a greenfield run, early phases are scaffolds (`pnpm create next-app`, Prisma schema, CI wiring) — no user surface yet. So the skill needs to classify each phase before it picks layer quotas:

- **user-facing** — phase delivers something a user clicks/types/sees. Full user-journey quotas.
- **integration** — phase delivers a subsystem that a *later phase* will expose (auth, API surface, data layer). The "consumer" in this phase is a technical caller, not a user. curl/jq checks are legitimate here and called `integration-probe` to name them honestly.
- **infrastructure** — scaffolding, migrations, CI, dev-env plumbing. No consumer within the run. Structural + a liveness smoke (does the dev server actually start?) is enough.

Classification source: phase name + description in `PLAN.json`. No planner change needed — the contract skill reads the phase it's contracting and decides. When in doubt, prefer user-facing: even a bare Next.js scaffold *can* have "visit `/`, see default page" — which is a better proof than `typecheck passes`.

## Draft — for eyeball, not committed

Proposed replacement for the opening ~150 lines of `create-contract/SKILL.md`. Everything below the "Step 3: Write the Contract" header stays as-is for now (schema rules, Pydantic validation). I'll tackle those on the second pass.

````markdown
---
name: create-contract
description: Create acceptance criteria contract for a phase before execution
context: fork
agent: contract-negotiator
---

# Create Phase Contract

You define the acceptance criteria that the REVIEWER will check after the EXECUTOR
finishes a phase. These criteria are the definition of "done" — if they pass, the
phase ships. If they fail, the executor has to fix.

## The one rule that matters

**Every criterion describes something a user (or, on pre-UI phases, a calling
subsystem) can observe.** Not "the API returned 200." Not "the function exists."
What the user sees, types, clicks, or is blocked from doing.

If a criterion passes and a real user would notice no difference in their
experience, the criterion tested the wrong thing.

## Step 1: Read the SPEC's user outcome

1. Read `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/SPEC.md`. Find the statements about what the user
   can do or see when this feature is done — the "User Outcome."
2. If the SPEC has no explicit user-outcome section, synthesize one from the
   Goal and Requirements, and write it back to SPEC.md under a `## User
   Outcome` heading so downstream phases inherit it. (This is a deliberate
   forcing function — without an outcome anchor, contracts drift technical.)
3. Every criterion you write must map back to one of those user-outcome
   statements. If you can't map it, it's probably testing plumbing the user
   doesn't care about.

## Step 2: Classify the phase

Read the phase's name and description in `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/PLAN.json`. Pick one:

| Phase kind | When to pick | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| `user-facing` | The phase produces a UI surface or a user-observable behavior change. | "Admin can manage users", "Add dark mode toggle", "Checkout flow" |
| `integration` | The phase delivers a subsystem that a later phase will expose to users. Not user-visible yet, but has a clear downstream consumer. | "Auth middleware", "tRPC router scaffold", "Background job queue" |
| `infrastructure` | Scaffolding, migrations, tooling, CI. No consumer within the run. | "Scaffold Next.js app", "Prisma schema + initial migration", "GitHub Actions CI" |

When in doubt, prefer `user-facing`. Greenfield scaffolds often still have a
trivial user-visible surface (dev server serves default page) — proving that
end-to-end is higher-value than a bare `typecheck passes`.

Record your choice as `phase_kind` in the contract JSON.

## The three layers

### `structural` — does it build and start?
Compile, lint, typecheck, subsystem liveness. Quick sanity, not the substance.

### `user-journey` — the main layer (was: `behavioral`)
A user does something and observes something. Written as a short narrative,
verified end-to-end through the real UI when possible.

**Good description shape:**
- "After logging in, the admin sees the User Management link in the sidebar."
- "When a user types an invalid email and submits, they see 'Please enter a
  valid email' below the field."
- "On opening the dashboard, the manager sees three cards showing total users,
  active users, and disabled users."

**Bad description shape:**
- "GET /api/x returns 200 with field y."
- "The `users` table has a `created_at` column."
- "The POST handler validates the payload."

For `integration` phases with no UI yet, "user" means the calling subsystem —
and the criterion should describe a contract the caller relies on.

### `edge-case` — the user is wrong or the system is degraded (was: `boundary`)
The user submits bad data, has no permission, or hits a missing resource, and
sees something they can act on.

**Good:** "When the admin submits a form with an email already registered, they
see 'This email is already in use' near the email field and the form stays open."

**Bad:** "POST returns 409."

## Layer quotas by phase kind

| Phase kind | structural | user-journey | edge-case | total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `user-facing` | max 2 | min 3 | min 1 | max 8 |
| `integration` | max 2 | min 2 *(as contract probes, curl-legit)* | min 1 | max 6 |
| `infrastructure` | min 1, max 3 *(includes a liveness smoke)* | — | — | max 4 |

**Final-phase rule still applies:** if this is the highest-numbered phase in
PLAN.json, at least one `user-journey` criterion must exercise the full e2e
framework (Playwright/Cypress/Detox/pytest --e2e) against the running system.
Final phases cannot be classified `infrastructure` — if the run's last phase
has no user-visible delivery, the plan is wrong.

## Verification tool preference

Match the verification tool to what the criterion tests:

1. **Playwright / project e2e framework** — default for every `user-journey`
   and `edge-case` criterion on `user-facing` phases. The shell envelope stays:
   `Run: npx playwright test --grep "admin adds user" --reporter=line`.
2. **curl + jq** — legitimate for `integration` phases (there's no UI yet) and
   for pure-backend features (webhooks, cron, migrations).
3. **Build / lint / typecheck / migrate** — `structural` only.

If `test-harness.json` reports `e2e_framework: "none"` on a user-facing phase,
fall back to curl-shaped criteria *and* add a warning to `quality_gates`:
*"No e2e framework configured — user-journey criteria verified indirectly via
API; invest in Playwright before shipping."*

## Banned verification patterns

These test the wrong thing. Forbidden in `user-journey` and `edge-case`:

| Pattern | Why it's bad | What to use instead |
|---|---|---|
| `grep "def func" src/file.py` | String in source ≠ func works | Exercise the function through its real caller |
| `test -f src/routes/users.py` | File existing ≠ feature working | Hit the feature the file implements |
| `curl ... \| grep '"status":"ok"'` as user-journey | Server replied, not that any user saw anything | Playwright: navigate, assert visible text |
| `jq '.id'` as "feature works" on a user-facing phase | Response shape ≠ user experience | Playwright: create via UI, assert the row renders |
| `test "$HTTP_CODE" = "403"` as user-facing edge-case | Status codes are invisible to users | Playwright: navigate, assert "access denied" UI |
| `wc -l src/models.py` | Line count says nothing | Test the model via an actual caller |

## Worked examples

### `user-facing` phase: "Admin can manage users"

```json
{
  "phase_id": "04",
  "phase_kind": "user-facing",
  "objective": "An admin can add, edit, and disable users and see the results immediately on screen",
  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-04-01",
      "description": "Project compiles and the dev server serves the admin area",
      "verification": "Run: npm run build && (npm run dev &) && sleep 4 && curl -sf http://localhost:3000/admin",
      "layer": "structural"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-04-02",
      "description": "After logging in as admin and opening User Management, the admin sees existing users listed with their email and role",
      "verification": "Run: npx playwright test --grep 'admin sees user list' --reporter=line",
      "layer": "user-journey"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-04-03",
      "description": "The admin clicks 'Add User', fills email + role, submits, and sees the new row appear in the table without a full page reload",
      "verification": "Run: npx playwright test --grep 'admin adds user inline' --reporter=line",
      "layer": "user-journey"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-04-04",
      "description": "When the admin changes a user's role and saves, the row updates in place and the change persists across a page reload",
      "verification": "Run: npx playwright test --grep 'admin edits role persists' --reporter=line",
      "layer": "user-journey"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-04-05",
      "description": "When the admin submits a duplicate email, they see 'This email is already registered' next to the email field and the form stays open",
      "verification": "Run: npx playwright test --grep 'duplicate email inline error' --reporter=line",
      "layer": "edge-case"
    }
  ]
}
```

### `integration` phase: "Auth layer"

```json
{
  "phase_id": "02",
  "phase_kind": "integration",
  "objective": "Downstream features can require and receive an authenticated session",
  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-02-01",
      "description": "Project compiles and auth routes are registered",
      "verification": "Run: npm run build && curl -sf http://localhost:3000/api/auth/providers",
      "layer": "structural"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-02-02",
      "description": "A sign-in request with valid credentials returns a usable session token that downstream handlers can verify",
      "verification": "Run: TOKEN=$(curl -sf -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/auth/login -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{\"email\":\"admin@test.com\",\"password\":\"test123\"}' | jq -r .token) && curl -sfH \"Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN\" http://localhost:3000/api/me | jq -e '.email == \"admin@test.com\"'",
      "layer": "user-journey"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-02-03",
      "description": "A request without a valid token is rejected — downstream handlers can rely on authentication being enforced",
      "verification": "Run: HTTP=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://localhost:3000/api/me) && test \"$HTTP\" = \"401\"",
      "layer": "edge-case"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-02-04",
      "description": "A sign-in attempt for a disabled account is rejected",
      "verification": "Run: HTTP=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/auth/login -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{\"email\":\"disabled@test.com\",\"password\":\"test123\"}') && echo $HTTP | grep -qE '40[13]'",
      "layer": "edge-case"
    }
  ]
}
```

Note: no UI verification here — this phase has none. The next phase that exposes
a login screen gets real `user-journey` criteria via Playwright.

### `infrastructure` phase: "Scaffold Next.js + Prisma"

```json
{
  "phase_id": "01",
  "phase_kind": "infrastructure",
  "objective": "The base app scaffold is in place; the dev server runs; migrations apply cleanly",
  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-01-01",
      "description": "Project installs and type-checks without errors",
      "verification": "Run: pnpm install && pnpm typecheck",
      "layer": "structural"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-01-02",
      "description": "Prisma migrations apply against a clean database",
      "verification": "Run: pnpm prisma migrate reset --force --skip-seed && pnpm prisma migrate status | grep -q 'Database schema is up to date'",
      "layer": "structural"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-01-03",
      "description": "The dev server starts and the default page is served at /",
      "verification": "Run: (pnpm dev &) && sleep 5 && curl -sf http://localhost:3000/ | grep -q '<html'",
      "layer": "structural"
    }
  ]
}
```

Note: all `structural` — scaffolding has no user-journey. The liveness probe
(`curl /`) is counted as structural, not user-journey, because it proves the
subsystem is alive, not that any user interaction succeeded.
````

## Three things to decide before I do pass 2 (schema/rules/validation section)

1. **Layer rename in `CriterionLayer` enum** — go / no-go. If go: 3-line change, one backward-compat mapping for any existing contracts on disk. If no-go: the skill teaches `user-journey` / `edge-case` but JSON stores `behavioral` / `boundary`. That split is confusing to future maintainers.

2. **`phase_kind` field addition to `Contract` schema** — I added it to the examples. It's needed so the reviewer can enforce quotas and so later tooling (portal display, progress summaries) can differentiate. ~5-line addition to `contract_schema.py`.

3. **SPEC user-outcome writeback** — the draft has the contract skill *write back* a User Outcome section to `SPEC.md` if one doesn't exist. This is the clever bit that avoids touching `generate-spec` right now. But: is that OK with you? The alternative is having it fail-fast and demand the user rerun `generate-spec` first. The writeback means the contract skill becomes a soft forcing function for the SPEC over time.

Pick 1/2/3 yes-or-no, push back on anything in the draft, and I'll do pass 2 (schema rules + Pydantic validation section + banned-pattern integration) next.

content.text:
You're right, and I missed it. Unit tests don't fit `structural` (compile ≠ logic correct), they're not `user-journey` (no one sees them), and stuffing them under `quality_gates` makes them vibes — the current `quality_gates` field is plain strings, not executable, so the reviewer can't actually enforce them.

Clean fix: add `unit` as a fourth layer, sibling to structural. Same `Run:` envelope, same reviewer mechanism, but named honestly.

## Why `unit` deserves its own layer (not folded into structural)

- **Different failure mode.** Structural = "code compiles." Unit = "logic does what it says under isolated inputs." A phase can compile and lint clean while the new validator silently accepts empty strings.
- **Different role in the pyramid.** user-journey criteria prove the feature works today; unit criteria protect it against regression tomorrow. Both are "done," but they're defending different things.
- **TDD-friendly.** Your playbook already has a `test-driven-development` skill. Putting unit tests into acceptance_criteria means the executor sees "write these unit tests" as a hard gate, not a suggestion.

## Updated layer set

- `structural` — compile / lint / typecheck / migrate / dev server starts
- `unit` — new logic has fast, deterministic tests that exercise it in isolation *(NEW)*
- `user-journey` — a user (or calling subsystem on `integration` phases) does X and observes Y
- `edge-case` — user or subsystem hits a bad path and observes a graceful response

## Quotas per phase kind (revised)

| Phase kind | structural | unit | user-journey | edge-case | total |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `user-facing` | max 2 | min 1 *(if new logic)* | min 3 | min 1 | max 8 |
| `integration` | max 2 | **min 2** | min 2 *(contract probes)* | min 1 | max 8 |
| `infrastructure` | min 1, max 3 | — | — | — | max 4 |

**Why `integration` gets min 2 unit, not user-facing.** Integration phases are where most business logic actually lives (auth, transformations, validators). The API surface is narrow; unit tests are the *primary* safety net there, not a supplement. On user-facing phases, Playwright is doing most of the work and unit tests supplement it — one is usually enough unless the phase introduces meaningful pure logic (a custom sort, a complex form validator, a state machine).

**Why `infrastructure` gets zero unit.** Scaffolds are configuration, not logic. If you find yourself wanting unit tests in an infrastructure phase, the phase probably isn't actually infrastructure — re-classify.

## What a `unit` criterion looks like

```json
{
  "id": "AC-04-06",
  "description": "The user-form validator rejects empty email, invalid email format, and emails longer than 254 characters; accepts valid RFC-compliant emails",
  "verification": "Run: pnpm test -- src/users/validators.test.ts --run --reporter=default",
  "layer": "unit"
}
```

Note: the *description* still reads like user intent ("rejects empty email..."), just scoped to what the function does rather than what the user sees. Same "write it as behavior" discipline, different level of the stack.

## Worked-example update (user-facing phase)

The "Admin can manage users" example from before now has 6 criteria instead of 5:

1. `structural` — `npm run build && curl /admin`
2. **`unit` — the email-validator rejects/accepts per spec** *(NEW)*
3. `user-journey` — admin sees user list
4. `user-journey` — admin adds user, sees row appear
5. `user-journey` — admin edits role, change persists
6. `edge-case` — duplicate email shows inline error

Under the `max 8` cap. Room for a 7th `edge-case` (e.g. network failure on submit) if the phase warrants.

## Schema implication

`CriterionLayer` gains one value: `UNIT = "unit"`. So the total rename/add is:

```python
class CriterionLayer(str, Enum):
    STRUCTURAL = "structural"
    UNIT = "unit"                  # NEW
    USER_JOURNEY = "user-journey"  # was BEHAVIORAL
    EDGE_CASE = "edge-case"        # was BOUNDARY
```

Four-line change, plus the existing backward-compat mapping pattern (line 95-99 of `contract_schema.py`) extended for the rename.

## Also, a small correction to my earlier draft

My banned-patterns table flagged `curl ... | grep '"status":"ok"'` as bad for `user-journey` — correct for user-facing phases, but on `integration` phases where `user-journey` means "a calling subsystem," a curl-based contract probe IS the right verification. I'll clarify in pass 2 that the banned patterns apply *per phase kind*, not universally.

## Confirm before pass 2

- Add `unit` layer: yes / no?
- Quotas as proposed (min 1 unit on user-facing, min 2 on integration, none on infrastructure)?
- Anything else I missed? (Integration tests between two subsystems without a UI? Performance criteria? Accessibility criteria? I can flag these as "not first-class yet, goes in quality_gates as notes" unless you want them layered too.)

content.text: Confirmed. Let me check the validator and the rest of the schema so pass 2 lands cleanly, then draft.

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Validate Contract JSON against the Pydantic schema. Standalone — no imports beyond stdlib."""
3	import json
4	import re
5	import sys
6	
7	VALID_TOP_LEVEL_KEYS = {
8	    "phase_id",
9	    "phase_name",
10	    "objective",
11	    "acceptance_criteria",
12	    "quality_gates",
13	    "out_of_scope",
14	    "expected_files",
15	}
16	
17	VALID_LAYERS = {"structural", "behavioral", "boundary"}
18	
19	
20	def validate(path: str):
21	    errors = []
22	    warnings = []
23	
24	    with open(path) as f:
25	        data = json.load(f)
26	
27	    if not isinstance(data, dict):
28	        errors.append("Root must be a JSON object")
29	        return errors, warnings
30	
31	    # Reject unknown top-level keys
32	    unknown_keys = set(data.keys()) - VALID_TOP_LEVEL_KEYS
33	    if unknown_keys:
34	        errors.append(f"Unknown top-level keys: {sorted(unknown_keys)} — did you mean 'acceptance_criteria'?")
35	
36	    # Required fields
37	    for field in ("phase_id", "phase_name", "objective"):
38	        if field not in data:
39	            errors.append(f"Missing required field: '{field}'")
40	        elif not isinstance(data[field], str) or not data[field].strip():
41	            errors.append(f"'{field}' must be a non-empty string")
42	
43	    # acceptance_criteria
44	    if "acceptance_criteria" not in data:
45	        errors.append("Missing required field: 'acceptance_criteria'")
46	    else:
47	        ac = data["acceptance_criteria"]
48	        if not isinstance(ac, list):
49	            errors.append("'acceptance_criteria' must be a list")
50	        else:
51	            if len(ac) < 1:
52	                errors.append("'acceptance_criteria' must have at least 1 item")
53	            if len(ac) > 8:
54	                errors.append(f"'acceptance_criteria' has {len(ac)} items; maximum is 8")
55	
56	            structural_count = 0
57	            behavioral_count = 0
58	
59	            for i, criterion in enumerate(ac):
60	                prefix = f"acceptance_criteria[{i}]"
61	                if not isinstance(criterion, dict):
62	                    errors.append(f"{prefix}: must be an object")
63	                    continue
64	
65	                # id
66	                if "id" not in criterion:
67	                    errors.append(f"{prefix}: missing 'id'")
68	                elif not isinstance(criterion["id"], str):
69	                    errors.append(f"{prefix}.id: must be a string")
70	                elif not re.fullmatch(r"AC-\d+-\d+", criterion["id"]):
71	                    errors.append(f"{prefix}.id: '{criterion['id']}' does not match AC-\\d+-\\d+")
72	
73	                # description
74	                if "description" not in criterion:
75	                    errors.append(f"{prefix}: missing 'description'")
76	                elif not isinstance(criterion["description"], str) or not criterion["description"].strip():
77	                    errors.append(f"{prefix}.description: must be a non-empty string")
78	
79	                # verification
80	                if "verification" not in criterion:
81	                    errors.append(f"{prefix}: missing 'verification'")
82	                elif not isinstance(criterion["verification"], str):
83	                    errors.append(f"{prefix}.verification: must be a string")
84	                elif not criterion["verification"].startswith("Run: "):
85	                    errors.append(f"{prefix}.verification: must start with 'Run: ' (got '{criterion['verification'][:20]}...')")
86	
87	                # layer
88	                if "layer" not in criterion:
89	                    errors.append(f"{prefix}: missing 'layer'")
90	                elif criterion["layer"] not in VALID_LAYERS:
91	                    errors.append(f"{prefix}.layer: '{criterion['layer']}' must be one of {sorted(VALID_LAYERS)}")
92	                else:
93	                    if criterion["layer"] == "structural":
94	                        structural_count += 1
95	                    elif criterion["layer"] == "behavioral":
96	                        behavioral_count += 1
97	
98	            if structural_count > 2:
99	                errors.append(f"Too many structural criteria: {structural_count} (max 2)")
100	            if behavioral_count < 1:
101	                errors.append("At least 1 behavioral criterion is required")
102	
103	    # Optional: quality_gates
104	    if "quality_gates" in data:
105	        qg = data["quality_gates"]
106	        if not isinstance(qg, list):
107	            errors.append("'quality_gates' must be a list")
108	        else:
109	            for i, item in enumerate(qg):
110	                if not isinstance(item, str):
111	                    errors.append(f"quality_gates[{i}]: must be a string")
112	
113	    # Optional: out_of_scope
114	    if "out_of_scope" in data:
115	        oos = data["out_of_scope"]
116	        if not isinstance(oos, list):
117	            errors.append("'out_of_scope' must be a list")
118	        else:
119	            for i, item in enumerate(oos):
120	                if not isinstance(item, str):
121	                    errors.append(f"out_of_scope[{i}]: must be a string")
122	
123	    # Optional: expected_files
124	    if "expected_files" in data:
125	        ef = data["expected_files"]
126	        if not isinstance(ef, list):
127	            errors.append("'expected_files' must be a list")
128	        else:
129	            for i, item in enumerate(ef):
130	                prefix = f"expected_files[{i}]"
131	                if not isinstance(item, dict):
132	                    errors.append(f"{prefix}: must be an object")
133	                    continue
134	                if "path" not in item:
135	                    errors.append(f"{prefix}: missing 'path'")
136	                elif not isinstance(item["path"], str):
137	                    errors.append(f"{prefix}.path: must be a string")
138	                if "change" not in item:
139	                    errors.append(f"{prefix}: missing 'change'")
140	                elif not isinstance(item["change"], str):
141	                    errors.append(f"{prefix}.change: must be a string")
142	
143	    return errors, warnings
144	
145	
146	def main():
147	    if len(sys.argv) != 2:
148	        print(f"Usage: {sys.argv[0]} <json-file>", file=sys.stderr)
149	        sys.exit(1)
150	
151	    try:
152	        errors, warnings = validate(sys.argv[1])
153	    except json.JSONDecodeError as e:
154	        print(f"VALIDATION FAILED: Invalid JSON: {e}")
155	        sys.exit(1)
156	    except FileNotFoundError:
157	        print(f"VALIDATION FAILED: File not found: {sys.argv[1]}")
158	        sys.exit(1)
159	
160	    for w in warnings:
161	        print(f"  WARNING: {w}")
162	
163	    if errors:
164	        print("VALIDATION FAILED:")
165	        for e in errors:

170	        sys.exit(0)

174	    main()

Pass 2 touches four files. Drafting all of them inline — no edits yet.

## A. `create-contract/SKILL.md` — Step 3 through end

Replaces lines 134-300 of the current file. Picks up after "Worked examples" from pass 1.

````markdown
## Step 3: Build verification commands

The rule: match the tool to what the criterion claims to test.

### For `user-journey` and `edge-case` on `user-facing` phases
Playwright (or the project's configured e2e framework) is the default. Never curl.

```bash
Run: npx playwright test --grep "admin adds user inline" --reporter=line
```

The Playwright spec file is a deliverable of the phase — the executor writes it
alongside the implementation. Reference the test name in your criterion's
`description` so the connection is obvious ("The admin clicks 'Add User'..." →
spec file named `admin-adds-user-inline.spec.ts`).

### For `user-journey` and `edge-case` on `integration` phases
curl + jq is legitimate. The "user" here is a calling subsystem; the contract
probe is the equivalent of a UI interaction.

```bash
Run: TOKEN=$(curl -sf -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/auth/login \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"email":"admin@test.com","password":"test123"}' | jq -r .token) && \
  curl -sfH "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" http://localhost:3000/api/me | \
  jq -e '.email == "admin@test.com"'
```

If `test-harness.json` specifies `auth.login_command` and `base_url`, use them
verbatim — don't hand-roll login flows.

### For `unit`
The project's unit test runner, scoped to the files added in this phase:

```bash
Run: pnpm test -- src/users/validators.test.ts --run --reporter=default
Run: python -m pytest tests/unit/test_users.py -v
Run: go test ./internal/users -run TestUserValidator
```

Scope the command so the reviewer can tell which tests the criterion gates. A
phase-wide `pnpm test` is too coarse — a regression in another module would
silently fail this criterion.

### For `structural`
Build, lint, typecheck, migrate, dev-server-starts. One command per criterion:

```bash
Run: npm run build
Run: pnpm typecheck
Run: pnpm prisma migrate status | grep -q 'up to date'
Run: (npm run dev &) && sleep 4 && curl -sf http://localhost:3000/
```

### If `test-harness.json` exists, use it

| Harness field | How to use |
|---|---|
| `base_url` | Base for curl/Playwright page.goto |
| `auth.login_command` | Run before authenticated criteria |
| `seed.users` | Reference by email/role in criterion descriptions |
| `e2e_framework.run_command` | Every user-facing phase MUST use this for user-journey criteria |
| `health_checks` | One may become a structural criterion |

### If `test-harness.json` reports `e2e_framework: "none"`

On `user-facing` phases: fall back to curl-shaped `user-journey` criteria AND
add this to `quality_gates`: `"No e2e framework configured — user-journey
criteria verified indirectly via API; invest in Playwright before shipping to
real users."`

## Step 4: Write the contract

Write `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/contracts/phase-{PHASE_ID}.json`:

```json
{
  "phase_id": "04",
  "phase_name": "User Management",
  "phase_kind": "user-facing",
  "objective": "An admin can add, edit, and disable users and see the results immediately on screen",
  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-04-01",
      "description": "Project compiles and the admin area is reachable",
      "verification": "Run: npm run build && (npm run dev &) && sleep 4 && curl -sf http://localhost:3000/admin",
      "layer": "structural"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-04-02",
      "description": "The user-form validator rejects empty email, invalid email format, and emails over 254 chars; accepts RFC-compliant emails",
      "verification": "Run: pnpm test -- src/users/validators.test.ts --run",
      "layer": "unit"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-04-03",
      "description": "After logging in as admin and opening User Management, the admin sees existing users listed with email and role",
      "verification": "Run: npx playwright test --grep 'admin sees user list' --reporter=line",
      "layer": "user-journey"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-04-04",
      "description": "The admin clicks 'Add User', fills email + role, submits, and sees the new row appear in the table without a page reload",
      "verification": "Run: npx playwright test --grep 'admin adds user inline' --reporter=line",
      "layer": "user-journey"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-04-05",
      "description": "The admin changes a user's role and saves; the row updates in place and persists across reload",
      "verification": "Run: npx playwright test --grep 'admin edits role persists' --reporter=line",
      "layer": "user-journey"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-04-06",
      "description": "When the admin submits a duplicate email, they see 'This email is already registered' next to the email field and the form stays open",
      "verification": "Run: npx playwright test --grep 'duplicate email inline error' --reporter=line",
      "layer": "edge-case"
    }
  ],
  "quality_gates": [
    "All new/modified files compile without errors",
    "No regressions in existing functionality",
    "Code follows project conventions"
  ],
  "out_of_scope": ["Password reset flow", "Bulk user import"],
  "expected_files": [
    {"path": "src/users/validators.ts", "change": "Email and role validators"},
    {"path": "src/users/validators.test.ts", "change": "Unit tests for validators"},
    {"path": "src/app/admin/users/page.tsx", "change": "User management screen"},
    {"path": "tests/e2e/admin-users.spec.ts", "change": "Playwright specs for all user-journey criteria"}
  ]
}
```

## Rules

<rules>
<rule>Every criterion's `description` must read as user intent (or, on integration phases, calling-subsystem intent) — not as implementation detail. If removing the word "user" from the description breaks the sentence, you're writing it right.</rule>
<rule>Every criterion must map to a User Outcome statement in SPEC.md. If SPEC.md has no User Outcome section, synthesize one from Goal + Requirements and write it back to SPEC.md before proceeding.</rule>
<rule>`phase_kind` is required. One of: `user-facing`, `integration`, `infrastructure`.</rule>
<rule>Layer quotas by phase_kind (enforced by validator):
  - `user-facing`: max 2 structural, min 3 user-journey, min 1 edge-case, min 1 unit STRONGLY recommended when the phase adds pure logic (validators, transformers, state machines) — validator warns if 0.
  - `integration`: max 2 structural, min 2 unit, min 2 user-journey (as contract probes), min 1 edge-case.
  - `infrastructure`: min 1 structural, max 3 structural, zero of everything else. Total max 3.</rule>
<rule>`user-facing` and `integration` phases: max 8 criteria total. `infrastructure`: max 3 total.</rule>
<rule>Every `verification` MUST start with `Run: ` followed by an executable shell command.</rule>
<rule>On `user-facing` phases, `user-journey` and `edge-case` verifications MUST use the project's e2e framework (Playwright/Cypress/Detox/pytest --e2e). curl/jq is forbidden for these layers on user-facing phases.</rule>
<rule>On `integration` phases, `user-journey` criteria may use curl/jq — the "user" is a calling subsystem, not a human.</rule>
<rule>On the final phase (highest-numbered in PLAN.json), at least one user-journey criterion MUST use the project's e2e framework end-to-end. The final phase cannot be classified `infrastructure`.</rule>
<rule>`unit` verifications MUST be scoped to files this phase adds or modifies — not the whole suite. The reviewer needs to tell which tests the criterion actually gates.</rule>
<rule>ID format: `AC-{PHASE_ID}-{NN}` (e.g., AC-04-01).</rule>
<rule>`objective` must start with a verb the user performs, not the system performs. "An admin can manage users..." not "Implement CRUD endpoints...".</rule>
<rule>Expected_files must include any new test files (e2e specs, unit test files) — the reviewer uses this for scope checks and the executor treats it as a deliverable list.</rule>
</rules>

## Banned verification patterns

These test the wrong thing. Forbidden on the layers shown.

| Pattern | Banned on | Why | Use instead |
|---|---|---|---|
| `grep "def func" src/file.py` | all layers | String in source ≠ func works | Exercise the function through its real caller |
| `test -f src/routes/users.py` | all layers | File existing ≠ feature working | Hit the feature the file implements |
| `wc -l src/models.py` | all layers | Line count says nothing | Test the model through its caller |
| `curl ... \| grep '"status":"ok"'` | user-journey, edge-case on `user-facing` | Server replied, not that any user saw anything | Playwright: navigate, assert visible text |
| `jq '.id'` as "feature works" | user-journey, edge-case on `user-facing` | Response shape ≠ user experience | Playwright: create via UI, assert the row renders |
| `test "$HTTP_CODE" = "403"` | edge-case on `user-facing` | Status codes are invisible to users | Playwright: navigate, assert "access denied" UI |
| `pnpm test` (no file scope) | unit | A regression in another module would silently fail this | Scope to the files this phase touches |

The litmus test on `user-facing` phases: *"If this verification passes, would a
USER notice something is working?"* If no, it's the wrong layer or the wrong tool.

<constraints>
<constraint>Output ONLY the JSON file — no markdown, no commentary</constraint>
<constraint>File path MUST be ${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/contracts/phase-{PHASE_ID}.json</constraint>
</constraints>

## Validation (MANDATORY)

After writing the JSON file, validate it:

```bash
python ${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}/scripts/validate_schema.py ${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/contracts/phase-$ARGUMENTS.json
```

If validation fails, fix the JSON and re-run until it passes.
````

## B. `contract_schema.py` diff

```python
# Rename + add enum values
class CriterionLayer(str, Enum):
    STRUCTURAL = "structural"
    UNIT = "unit"                   # NEW
    USER_JOURNEY = "user-journey"   # was BEHAVIORAL
    EDGE_CASE = "edge-case"         # was BOUNDARY


class PhaseKind(str, Enum):         # NEW
    USER_FACING = "user-facing"
    INTEGRATION = "integration"
    INFRASTRUCTURE = "infrastructure"


class AcceptanceCriterion(BaseModel):
    id: str = Field(..., description="Unique ID like AC-01-01")
    description: str = Field(..., description="User-framed intent")
    verification: str = Field(..., description="Executable command (Run: ...)")
    layer: CriterionLayer = CriterionLayer.USER_JOURNEY  # default renamed


class Contract(BaseModel):
    phase_id: str
    phase_name: str
    phase_kind: PhaseKind            # NEW — required
    objective: str = Field(..., description="Starts with a verb the user performs")
    acceptance_criteria: list[AcceptanceCriterion] = Field(
        ..., min_length=1, max_length=8,
    )
    # ... rest unchanged


# In Contract.load(), extend the existing backward-compat block:
@classmethod
def load(cls, path: Path) -> Optional["Contract"]:
    if not path.exists():
        return None
    try:
        raw = json.loads(path.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
        for criterion in raw.get("acceptance_criteria", []):
            # Existing: category → layer mapping (keep)
            if "category" in criterion and "layer" not in criterion:
                category = criterion.pop("category")
                criterion["layer"] = {
                    "functional": "user-journey",   # updated target
                    "quality": "structural",
                }.get(category, "user-journey")
            # NEW: old layer values → new layer values
            if criterion.get("layer") == "behavioral":
                criterion["layer"] = "user-journey"
            elif criterion.get("layer") == "boundary":
                criterion["layer"] = "edge-case"
        # NEW: default phase_kind on legacy contracts (best guess)
        if "phase_kind" not in raw:
            raw["phase_kind"] = "user-facing"
        return cls.model_validate(raw)
    except Exception:
        return None


# Update Contract.json_template() to reflect the new shape — shown in draft A above
```

```python
    "quality_gates", "out_of_scope", "expected_files",
}

VALID_LAYERS = {"structural", "unit", "user-journey", "edge-case"}
VALID_PHASE_KINDS = {"user-facing", "integration", "infrastructure"}

# Inside validate():
# Require phase_kind
if "phase_kind" not in data:
    errors.append("Missing required field: 'phase_kind' (user-facing | integration | infrastructure)")
elif data["phase_kind"] not in VALID_PHASE_KINDS:
    errors.append(f"'phase_kind' must be one of {sorted(VALID_PHASE_KINDS)}")

# Replace the layer-counting block with phase-kind-aware quotas:
phase_kind = data.get("phase_kind")
counts = {"structural": 0, "unit": 0, "user-journey": 0, "edge-case": 0}
for criterion in ac:
    layer = criterion.get("layer")
    if layer in counts:
        counts[layer] += 1

if phase_kind == "user-facing":
    if len(ac) > 8:
        errors.append(f"user-facing phase: max 8 criteria (got {len(ac)})")
    if counts["structural"] > 2:
        errors.append(f"user-facing: max 2 structural (got {counts['structural']})")
    if counts["user-journey"] < 3:
        errors.append(f"user-facing: min 3 user-journey (got {counts['user-journey']})")
    if counts["edge-case"] < 1:
        errors.append(f"user-facing: min 1 edge-case (got {counts['edge-case']})")
    if counts["unit"] == 0:
        warnings.append("user-facing: no unit criterion — if the phase adds pure logic (validators, transformers), add at least one")

elif phase_kind == "integration":
    if len(ac) > 8:
        errors.append(f"integration phase: max 8 criteria (got {len(ac)})")
    if counts["structural"] > 2:
        errors.append(f"integration: max 2 structural (got {counts['structural']})")
    if counts["unit"] < 2:
        errors.append(f"integration: min 2 unit (got {counts['unit']})")
    if counts["user-journey"] < 2:
        errors.append(f"integration: min 2 user-journey as contract probes (got {counts['user-journey']})")
    if counts["edge-case"] < 1:
        errors.append(f"integration: min 1 edge-case (got {counts['edge-case']})")

elif phase_kind == "infrastructure":
    if len(ac) > 3:
        errors.append(f"infrastructure phase: max 3 criteria (got {len(ac)})")
    if counts["structural"] < 1:
        errors.append("infrastructure: min 1 structural")
    if counts["structural"] > 3:
        errors.append(f"infrastructure: max 3 structural (got {counts['structural']})")
    for bad_layer in ("unit", "user-journey", "edge-case"):
        if counts[bad_layer] > 0:
            errors.append(f"infrastructure: no {bad_layer} criteria allowed (got {counts[bad_layer]})")

# Backward compat: accept old layer values for existing contracts
_layer_renames = {"behavioral": "user-journey", "boundary": "edge-case"}
for criterion in ac:
    if criterion.get("layer") in _layer_renames:
        warnings.append(f"{criterion['id']}: layer '{criterion['layer']}' is deprecated; use '{_layer_renames[criterion['layer']]}'")
```

## D. `review-phase/SKILL.md` tiny diff

Two rule lines need updating:

```diff
- <rule>Verdict is "fail" if ANY behavioral or boundary criterion has verdict "pass" but EMPTY evidence — you cannot declare pass without proof</rule>
+ <rule>Verdict is "fail" if ANY user-journey, edge-case, or unit criterion has verdict "pass" but EMPTY evidence — you cannot declare pass without proof</rule>

- <rule>Every behavioral/boundary criteria_results entry MUST include at least one evidence entry with the actual command run and its output — structural criteria may pass without evidence</rule>
+ <rule>Every user-journey, edge-case, and unit criteria_results entry MUST include at least one evidence entry with the actual command run and its output — structural criteria may pass without evidence</rule>
```

That's it for review-phase. The reviewer's mechanics (run `Run:` commands, record stdout) work unchanged — it doesn't care about layer semantics.

## Implementation order (one cohesive commit)

1. `contract_schema.py` — enum rename + add, `phase_kind` field, backward-compat mapping extended.
2. `scripts/validate_schema.py` (inside `create-contract/`) — phase-kind-aware quotas, new layer set, accept `phase_kind` top-level key.
3. `create-contract/SKILL.md` — full rewrite (pass 1 opening + pass 2).
4. `review-phase/SKILL.md` — two-rule diff.
5. Smoke-test: load an existing contract file (old `behavioral`/`boundary` values) through `Contract.load()` — should still parse with the backward-compat mapping.

## Open questions

- **`phase_kind` default on legacy contracts.** I'm defaulting to `user-facing` in `Contract.load()` backward-compat. That's the safest guess (tightest quotas, loudest feedback if something looks off). You prefer `integration` or fail-closed?
- **Unit warning vs error on user-facing phases.** I set "0 unit on user-facing" as a *warning*, not error — because some pure-UI phases legitimately have no new pure logic. If you want it as a hard error with an opt-out flag (`"no_unit_justified": "pure UI composition"`), say so.
- **Planner awareness.** The planner (`create-plan` skill) currently produces phase metadata; does it know about `phase_kind`? If not, the contract skill has to classify from phase name/description on every run. Acceptable for now, but flagging in case you want to push `phase_kind` up to the plan level.

Green light on the four-file change, or push back on any of the three open questions first?

message.content:
Green.

But flagging Planner awareness is great. Should we let the planner tune to be also more focused on user-output? Each phase must produce some visible change for an end user? And if not, the phase is too small. Then again, sometimes there is just "boring" work to do. But we actially observed the planner blowing up the number of phases unnecessarily (8 phases for a simple API change). So this is probably grounded in truth. Implement the current thing first, lets discuss the other stuff later.

input.content:
---
name: Planner phase-count tuning follow-up
description: Planner inflates phase count (observed 8 phases for a simple API change); tune after contract reframe ships
type: project
---

Follow-up surfaced during the contract-skill UX reframe, queued behind the orchestrator skill work.

**The observation:** The Dirigent planner produces too many phases for the work at hand. User reported 8 phases for a simple API change. Root cause unconfirmed but likely a combination of (a) no upper bound relative to spec complexity, (b) no forcing function that each phase justify itself with a user-visible delta.

**Why it matters:** Phase count drives contract count, review count, commit count, token spend. Every fake phase boundary is a coherence-loss point where the sub-agent re-infers context from scratch. Ties directly back to the "steps lose focus" complaint that started the supervisor conversation — more phases = more context resets = more drift.

**Grounded, not vibes:** The new `phase_kind` field from the contract reframe gives us signal: if a run produces mostly `infrastructure` phases with one lonely `user-facing` at the end, the planner over-sliced.

**Possible moves (to discuss, not commit):**
1. Teach planner that each phase must either (a) deliver a user-visible delta or (b) be classifiable as `infrastructure` / `integration` with an explicit justification ("cannot merge with next phase because..."). No free lunch for splitting phases.
2. Cap phase count relative to spec size — e.g. "≤3 phases unless the spec has >N requirements" or similar heuristic.
3. Force-merge adjacent same-kind phases (two consecutive `infrastructure` phases should almost always be one).
4. Rubric: after planning, ask "would a human reviewer accept these as separate PRs, or would they ask to squash?"

**How to apply:** Do not bring this up until both the contract reframe and the orchestrator skill work have shipped or been explicitly shelved. When ready, open with the 8-phases-for-an-API-change anecdote as the concrete pain point — it's more grounded than the abstract argument. Check the `create-plan` skill first before proposing changes.

**Do not confuse with:** the orchestrator-skill follow-up (that one is about replacing the Python control plane; this one is about the planner's judgment about phase granularity). Both are queued but orthogonal.

content.content:
1	---
2	name: contract-negotiator
3	description: Create phase acceptance criteria contracts. Probes the test infrastructure to write criteria with executable verification commands that actually work.
4	model: sonnet
5	effort: high
6	disallowedTools: Edit, Agent
7	---
8	
9	You negotiate the "definition of done" between implementation and review. Your contracts must yield REAL tests.
10	
11	## Your Goal
12	
13	Write acceptance criteria where every behavioral verification command is EXECUTABLE. The reviewer will run these commands literally — if they don't work, the review fails, the fix loop burns tokens, and nothing ships.
14	
15	## Process
16	
17	1. Read `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/PLAN.json` to find the phase
18	2. Read `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/SPEC.md` for feature context
19	2b. **Read `./ARCHITECTURE.md`** from the target repo root (if it exists). Extract: e2e framework name and run command, test directory structure and naming conventions, dev-server startup command, CI test commands that are known to work. This prevents inventing verification commands that don't match the repo's infrastructure.
20	3. Read `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/test-harness.json` for test infrastructure (base_url, auth, seed data, health checks)
21	3b. **Detect if this is the final phase**: count all phase IDs in PLAN.json. If this phase's ID is numerically the highest, it is the final phase. Final phases require at least one behavioral criterion using the e2e run command from `./ARCHITECTURE.md` or `test-harness.json` `e2e_framework.run_command`.
22	4. **PROBE the environment**: Before writing a verification command, try a simpler version to confirm it's plausible
23	   - Can curl reach the base_url? Try: `curl -sf {base_url}/health || echo "NOT REACHABLE"`
24	   - What test runner is available? Check: `which pytest`, `npx jest --version`, `go test --help`
25	   - Are ports open? Check: `lsof -i :{port} 2>/dev/null | head -3`
26	   - Is the e2e framework installed? Check: `npx playwright --version 2>/dev/null`, `npx cypress --version 2>/dev/null`, `detox --version 2>/dev/null`
27	5. Write the contract JSON
28	6. **VALIDATE**: Run `python ${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}/scripts/validate_schema.py ${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/contracts/phase-{PHASE_ID}.json`
29	7. Fix any validation errors and re-run until it passes
30	
31	## Contract JSON Schema (Pydantic-validated — EXACT field names required)
32	
33	```json
34	{
35	  "phase_id": "01",
36	  "phase_name": "Phase Name",
37	  "objective": "One sentence: what this phase achieves",
38	  "acceptance_criteria": [
39	    {
40	      "id": "AC-{PHASE_ID}-01",
41	      "description": "What must be true",
42	      "verification": "Run: <executable shell command>",
43	      "layer": "structural|behavioral|boundary"
44	    }
45	  ],
46	  "quality_gates": ["All new/modified files compile without errors", "No regressions", "Code follows conventions"],
47	  "out_of_scope": ["What this phase does NOT cover"],
48	  "expected_files": [{"path": "src/foo.py", "change": "Add new class"}]
49	}
50	```
51	
52	## Hard Constraints (Pydantic rejects violations)
53	
54	- Field name is `acceptance_criteria` — NOT `criteria`, `tests`, or `checks`
55	- Field name is `objective` — NOT `description` or `verification_strategy`
56	- Each criterion `id`: format `AC-{PHASE_ID}-{NN}` (e.g., AC-01-01)
57	- Each `verification` MUST start with `"Run: "`
58	- `layer` MUST be one of EXACTLY these 3 strings: `"structural"`, `"behavioral"`, `"boundary"`
59	  - NOT `"unit"`, `"code_quality"`, `"integration"`, `"functional"`, or any other value
60	- **Max 8 criteria total**. Min 1.
61	- Max 2 structural, min 3 behavioral, min 1 boundary
62	- `expected_files` entries MUST be objects: `{"path": "src/foo.py", "change": "description"}` — NOT plain strings
63	
64	## Fallback Strategy
65	
66	If test harness is NOT running (curl fails):
67	- Use the project's test runner for behavioral verification instead of curl
68	- Python: `Run: python -m pytest tests/test_feature.py -v -k "test_name"`
69	- Node: `Run: npx jest --testPathPattern="feature" --verbose`
70	- Go: `Run: go test ./pkg/feature/... -v -run TestName`

file.content:
---
name: contract-negotiator
description: Create phase acceptance criteria contracts. Probes the test infrastructure to write criteria with executable verification commands that actually work.
model: sonnet
effort: high
disallowedTools: Edit, Agent
---

You negotiate the "definition of done" between implementation and review. Your contracts must yield REAL tests.

## Your Goal

Write acceptance criteria where every behavioral verification command is EXECUTABLE. The reviewer will run these commands literally — if they don't work, the review fails, the fix loop burns tokens, and nothing ships.

## Process

1. Read `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/PLAN.json` to find the phase
2. Read `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/SPEC.md` for feature context
2b. **Read `./ARCHITECTURE.md`** from the target repo root (if it exists). Extract: e2e framework name and run command, test directory structure and naming conventions, dev-server startup command, CI test commands that are known to work. This prevents inventing verification commands that don't match the repo's infrastructure.
3. Read `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/test-harness.json` for test infrastructure (base_url, auth, seed data, health checks)
3b. **Detect if this is the final phase**: count all phase IDs in PLAN.json. If this phase's ID is numerically the highest, it is the final phase. Final phases require at least one behavioral criterion using the e2e run command from `./ARCHITECTURE.md` or `test-harness.json` `e2e_framework.run_command`.
4. **PROBE the environment**: Before writing a verification command, try a simpler version to confirm it's plausible
   - Can curl reach the base_url? Try: `curl -sf {base_url}/health || echo "NOT REACHABLE"`
   - What test runner is available? Check: `which pytest`, `npx jest --version`, `go test --help`
   - Are ports open? Check: `lsof -i :{port} 2>/dev/null | head -3`
   - Is the e2e framework installed? Check: `npx playwright --version 2>/dev/null`, `npx cypress --version 2>/dev/null`, `detox --version 2>/dev/null`
5. Write the contract JSON
6. **VALIDATE**: Run `python ${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}/scripts/validate_schema.py ${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/contracts/phase-{PHASE_ID}.json`
7. Fix any validation errors and re-run until it passes

## Contract JSON Schema (Pydantic-validated — EXACT field names required)

```json
{
  "phase_id": "01",
  "phase_name": "Phase Name",
  "objective": "One sentence: what this phase achieves",
  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-{PHASE_ID}-01",
      "description": "What must be true",
      "verification": "Run: <executable shell command>",
      "layer": "structural|behavioral|boundary"
    }
  ],
  "quality_gates": ["All new/modified files compile without errors", "No regressions", "Code follows conventions"],
  "out_of_scope": ["What this phase does NOT cover"],
  "expected_files": [{"path": "src/foo.py", "change": "Add new class"}]
}
```

## Hard Constraints (Pydantic rejects violations)

- Field name is `acceptance_criteria` — NOT `criteria`, `tests`, or `checks`
- Field name is `objective` — NOT `description` or `verification_strategy`
- Each criterion `id`: format `AC-{PHASE_ID}-{NN}` (e.g., AC-01-01)
- Each `verification` MUST start with `"Run: "`
- `layer` MUST be one of EXACTLY these 3 strings: `"structural"`, `"behavioral"`, `"boundary"`
  - NOT `"unit"`, `"code_quality"`, `"integration"`, `"functional"`, or any other value
- **Max 8 criteria total**. Min 1.
- Max 2 structural, min 3 behavioral, min 1 boundary
- `expected_files` entries MUST be objects: `{"path": "src/foo.py", "change": "description"}` — NOT plain strings

## Fallback Strategy

If test harness is NOT running (curl fails):
- Use the project's test runner for behavioral verification instead of curl
- Python: `Run: python -m pytest tests/test_feature.py -v -k "test_name"`
- Node: `Run: npx jest --testPathPattern="feature" --verbose`
- Go: `Run: go test ./pkg/feature/... -v -run TestName`

## MANDATORY Post-Write Validation

After writing the contract JSON, you MUST run:
```bash
python ${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}/scripts/validate_schema.py ${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/contracts/phase-{PHASE_ID}.json
```
If it fails, read the errors, fix the JSON, write again, and re-validate. Do NOT stop until VALIDATION PASSED.
