toolUseResult.originalFile:
"""
Plan schema — typed representation of PLAN.json.

Uses pydantic for validation, serialization, and deserialization.
"""

import json
import logging
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Optional

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


class Task(BaseModel):
    """A single executable task within a phase."""
    id: str
    name: str
    description: str = ""
    files_to_create: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    files_to_modify: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    depends_on: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    model: str = ""
    effort: str = ""
    test_level: str = ""  # "", "L1", "L2"
    convention_skills: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)  # e.g. ["ruby-code-writing", "form-builder"]
    relevant_req_ids: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)  # e.g. ["R3", "R7"] from SPEC.compact.json


class Phase(BaseModel):
    """A group of related tasks executed sequentially."""
    id: str
    name: str
    description: str = ""
    tasks: list[Task] = Field(default_factory=list)

    def model_post_init(self, __context):
        # Normalize: accept both "id" and "phase" field names
        if not self.id and hasattr(self, "phase"):
            self.id = str(getattr(self, "phase"))


class Plan(BaseModel):
    """The full execution plan for a dirigent run."""
    title: str = "Untitled"
    summary: str = ""
    phases: list[Phase] = Field(default_factory=list)
    estimated_complexity: str = "medium"
    risks: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    assumptions: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    out_of_scope: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)

    @property
    def total_tasks(self) -> int:
        return sum(len(p.tasks) for p in self.phases)

    @property
    def all_tasks(self) -> list[tuple[Task, Phase]]:
        """Flat list of (task, phase) in execution order."""
        return [(t, p) for p in self.phases for t in p.tasks]

    def task_position(self, task_id: str) -> Optional[dict]:
        """Position info for a task: index, total, phase, prev/next."""
        tasks = self.all_tasks
        idx = next((i for i, (t, _) in enumerate(tasks) if t.id == task_id), None)
        if idx is None:
            return None

        t, p = tasks[idx]
        info = {
            "index": idx + 1,
            "total": len(tasks),
            "phase_id": p.id,
            "phase_name": p.name,
            "total_phases": len(self.phases),
        }
        if idx > 0:
            prev_t, _ = tasks[idx - 1]
            info["prev_id"] = prev_t.id
            info["prev_name"] = prev_t.name
        if idx < len(tasks) - 1:
            next_t, _ = tasks[idx + 1]
            info["next_id"] = next_t.id
            info["next_name"] = next_t.name
        return info

    def save(self, path: Path):
        path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        path.write_text(
            self.model_dump_json(indent=2, exclude_none=True),
            encoding="utf-8",
        )

    @classmethod
    def load(cls, path: Path) -> Optional["Plan"]:
        """Load from PLAN.json. Returns None if missing or invalid."""
        if not path.exists():
            return None
        try:
            raw = json.loads(path.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
            # Normalize phase/task fields from common LLM output variations
            for p in raw.get("phases", []):
                if "phase" in p and "id" not in p:
                    p["id"] = str(p.pop("phase"))
                elif "id" in p:
                    p["id"] = str(p["id"])
                for t in p.get("tasks", []):
                    # title → name
                    if "title" in t and "name" not in t:
                        t["name"] = t.pop("title")
                    # files → files_to_modify (conservative default)
                    if "files" in t and "files_to_modify" not in t and "files_to_create" not in t:
                        t["files_to_modify"] = t.pop("files")
            return cls.model_validate(raw)
        except json.JSONDecodeError as e:
            logger.error("PLAN.json is not valid JSON: %s", e)
            return None
        except Exception as e:
            logger.error("PLAN.json validation failed: %s", e)
            return None

    @staticmethod
    def json_template() -> str:
        """JSON template string for the plan creation prompt."""
        return """{
  "title": "Feature-Titel",
  "summary": "Kurze Beschreibung was implementiert wird",
  "assumptions": [
    "Annahmen die du über die Codebase/das Feature machst",
    "z.B. 'Tests laufen mit pytest', 'API ist REST-basiert'"
  ],
  "out_of_scope": [
    "Was NICHT Teil dieses Plans ist",
    "z.B. 'Deployment/CI-Pipeline', 'Performance-Optimierung'"
  ],
  "phases": [
    {
      "id": "01",
      "name": "Phase-Name",
      "description": "Was in dieser Phase passiert",
      "tasks": [
        {
          "id": "01-01",
          "name": "Task-Name",
          "description": "Detaillierte Beschreibung was zu tun ist",
          "files_to_create": ["liste/von/neuen/dateien.ext"],
          "files_to_modify": ["liste/von/existierenden/dateien.ext"],
          "depends_on": [],
          "model": "sonnet|haiku|opus (welches Modell für diesen Task am besten ist)",
          "effort": "low|medium|high (wie viel Denkaufwand nötig ist)",
          "test_level": "L1|L2| (welches Test-Level nach diesem Task laufen soll, leer wenn kein Test nötig)",
          "convention_skills": ["skill-name-1", "skill-name-2 (aus .opencode/skills/, leer wenn keine)"],
          "relevant_req_ids": ["R3", "R7 (IDs aus SPEC.compact.json — welche Requirements dieser Task adressiert)"]
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "estimated_complexity": "low|medium|high",
  "risks": ["Liste von potentiellen Risiken"]
}"""

    INFRASTRUCTURE = "infrastructure"  # Scaffolding, migrations, tooling — no consumer in-run

"""

Uses pydantic for validation, serialization, and deserialization.

import json
import logging
from enum import Enum
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Optional

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

    """How a phase relates to the user.

    USER_FACING = "user-facing"        # Delivers UI surface or observable behavior change
    INTEGRATION = "integration"        # Subsystem a later phase will expose to users
    INFRASTRUCTURE = "infrastructure"  # Scaffolding, migrations, tooling — no consumer in-run

    """A single executable task within a phase."""
    id: str

    description: str = ""
    files_to_create: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    files_to_modify: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    depends_on: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    model: str = ""
    effort: str = ""
    test_level: str = ""  # "", "L1", "L2"
    convention_skills: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)  # e.g. ["ruby-code-writing", "form-builder"]
    relevant_req_ids: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)  # e.g. ["R3", "R7"] from SPEC.compact.json

    """A group of related tasks executed sequentially."""
    id: str
    name: str

    description: str = ""

"""
Plan schema — typed representation of PLAN.json.

Uses pydantic for validation, serialization, and deserialization.
"""

import json
import logging
from enum import Enum
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Optional

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


class PhaseKind(str, Enum):
    """How a phase relates to the user.

    Drives layer quotas in contracts and plan-level validation (max 1
    infrastructure, final phase ≠ infrastructure, etc).
    """
    USER_FACING = "user-facing"        # Delivers UI surface or observable behavior change
    INTEGRATION = "integration"        # Subsystem a later phase will expose to users
    INFRASTRUCTURE = "infrastructure"  # Scaffolding, migrations, tooling — no consumer in-run


class Task(BaseModel):
    """A single executable task within a phase."""
    id: str
    name: str
    description: str = ""
    files_to_create: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    files_to_modify: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    depends_on: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    model: str = ""
    effort: str = ""
    test_level: str = ""  # "", "L1", "L2"
    convention_skills: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)  # e.g. ["ruby-code-writing", "form-builder"]
    relevant_req_ids: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)  # e.g. ["R3", "R7"] from SPEC.compact.json


class Phase(BaseModel):
    """A group of related tasks executed sequentially."""
    id: str
    name: str
    kind: PhaseKind = Field(
        default=PhaseKind.USER_FACING,
        description="user-facing | integration | infrastructure — drives contract layer quotas",
    )
    description: str = ""
    merge_justification: str = Field(
        default="",
        description="One sentence: why this phase can't be merged with the next. Empty only if this is the last phase.",
    )
    tasks: list[Task] = Field(default_factory=list, max_length=4)

    def model_post_init(self, __context):
        # Normalize: accept both "id" and "phase" field names
        if not self.id and hasattr(self, "phase"):
            self.id = str(getattr(self, "phase"))


class Plan(BaseModel):
    """The full execution plan for a dirigent run."""
    title: str = "Untitled"
    summary: str = ""
    phases: list[Phase] = Field(default_factory=list, max_length=4)
    estimated_complexity: str = "medium"
    risks: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    assumptions: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    out_of_scope: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)

    @property
    def total_tasks(self) -> int:
        return sum(len(p.tasks) for p in self.phases)

    @property
    def all_tasks(self) -> list[tuple[Task, Phase]]:
        """Flat list of (task, phase) in execution order."""
        return [(t, p) for p in self.phases for t in p.tasks]

    def task_position(self, task_id: str) -> Optional[dict]:
        """Position info for a task: index, total, phase, prev/next."""
        tasks = self.all_tasks
        idx = next((i for i, (t, _) in enumerate(tasks) if t.id == task_id), None)
        if idx is None:
            return None

        t, p = tasks[idx]
        info = {
            "index": idx + 1,
            "total": len(tasks),
            "phase_id": p.id,
            "phase_name": p.name,
            "total_phases": len(self.phases),
        }
        if idx > 0:
            prev_t, _ = tasks[idx - 1]
            info["prev_id"] = prev_t.id
            info["prev_name"] = prev_t.name
        if idx < len(tasks) - 1:
            next_t, _ = tasks[idx + 1]
            info["next_id"] = next_t.id
            info["next_name"] = next_t.name
        return info

    def save(self, path: Path):
        path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        path.write_text(
            self.model_dump_json(indent=2, exclude_none=True),
            encoding="utf-8",
        )

    @classmethod
    def load(cls, path: Path) -> Optional["Plan"]:
        """Load from PLAN.json. Returns None if missing or invalid."""
        if not path.exists():
            return None
        try:
            raw = json.loads(path.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
            # Normalize phase/task fields from common LLM output variations
            for p in raw.get("phases", []):
                if "phase" in p and "id" not in p:
                    p["id"] = str(p.pop("phase"))
                elif "id" in p:
                    p["id"] = str(p["id"])
                # Pre-plan-tuning plans have no phase kind — default to user-facing
                # (tightest downstream contract quotas; loudest if misclassified)
                if "kind" not in p:
                    p["kind"] = "user-facing"
                for t in p.get("tasks", []):
                    # title → name
                    if "title" in t and "name" not in t:
                        t["name"] = t.pop("title")
                    # files → files_to_modify (conservative default)
                    if "files" in t and "files_to_modify" not in t and "files_to_create" not in t:
                        t["files_to_modify"] = t.pop("files")
            return cls.model_validate(raw)
        except json.JSONDecodeError as e:
            logger.error("PLAN.json is not valid JSON: %s", e)
            return None
        except Exception as e:
            logger.error("PLAN.json validation failed: %s", e)
            return None

    @staticmethod
    def json_template() -> str:
        """JSON template string for the plan creation prompt."""
        return """{
  "title": "Feature-Titel",
  "summary": "Kurze Beschreibung was implementiert wird",
  "assumptions": [
    "Annahmen die du über die Codebase/das Feature machst",
    "z.B. 'Tests laufen mit pytest', 'API ist REST-basiert'"
  ],
  "out_of_scope": [
    "Was NICHT Teil dieses Plans ist",
    "z.B. 'Deployment/CI-Pipeline', 'Performance-Optimierung'"
  ],
  "phases": [
    {
      "id": "01",
      "name": "Phase-Name",
      "description": "Was in dieser Phase passiert",
      "tasks": [
        {
          "id": "01-01",
          "name": "Task-Name",
          "description": "Detaillierte Beschreibung was zu tun ist",
          "files_to_create": ["liste/von/neuen/dateien.ext"],
          "files_to_modify": ["liste/von/existierenden/dateien.ext"],
          "depends_on": [],
          "model": "sonnet|haiku|opus (welches Modell für diesen Task am besten ist)",
          "effort": "low|medium|high (wie viel Denkaufwand nötig ist)",
          "test_level": "L1|L2| (welches Test-Level nach diesem Task laufen soll, leer wenn kein Test nötig)",
          "convention_skills": ["skill-name-1", "skill-name-2 (aus .opencode/skills/, leer wenn keine)"],
          "relevant_req_ids": ["R3", "R7 (IDs aus SPEC.compact.json — welche Requirements dieser Task adressiert)"]
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "estimated_complexity": "low|medium|high",
  "risks": ["Liste von potentiellen Risiken"]
}"""

input.old_string:
## Step 3: Create the Plan

Write `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/PLAN.json` with this exact format:

```json
{
  "title": "Feature-Titel",
  "summary": "Kurze Beschreibung was implementiert wird",
  "assumptions": ["Annahmen ueber die Codebase"],
  "out_of_scope": ["Was NICHT gemacht wird"],
  "phases": [
    {
      "id": "01",
      "name": "Phase-Name",
      "description": "Was in dieser Phase passiert",
      "tasks": [
        {
          "id": "01-01",
          "name": "Task-Name",
          "description": "Detaillierte Beschreibung was zu tun ist",
          "files_to_create": ["neue/dateien.ext"],
          "files_to_modify": ["existierende/dateien.ext"],
          "depends_on": [],
          "model": "sonnet",
          "effort": "medium",
          "test_level": "L1",
          "convention_skills": ["ruby-code-writing", "form-builder"],
          "relevant_req_ids": ["R3", "R7"]
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "estimated_complexity": "medium",
  "risks": ["Potentielle Risiken"]
}
```

## Rules

1. **Max 4 phases, max 4 tasks per phase**
2. Each task is atomic (does exactly one thing)
3. No dependencies between tasks within a phase
4. Tasks must be concrete and executable
5. If `BUSINESS_RULES.md` exists: all rules must be preserved
6. **model**: "haiku" for simple tasks, "sonnet" for standard, "opus" for complex architecture
7. **effort**: "low" for mechanical, "medium" for standard, "high" for complex logic
8. **test_level**: "L1" for unit tests/lint, "L2" for integration tests, empty if no testing needed
9. If `test-harness.json` exists: plan verification tasks that use its verification_commands and e2e_framework.run_command. The reviewer will use these to verify features end-to-end.
10. **Plan for maintainability** — the agent executing these tasks is the long-term maintainer of the codebase. Task descriptions should guide toward scalable patterns: clear interfaces, separation of concerns, explicit dependencies. Do not plan throwaway code.
11. **Plan for real verification** — each phase will be reviewed with executable verification commands. Do not plan tasks that "work" only in the sense that they compile. The reviewer will hit real endpoints and run real test suites.
12. **convention_skills**: If `.opencode/skills/` exists, tag each task with the skill names the coder should load. Be specific — a task creating a Ruby form object needs `["ruby-code-writing", "form-builder"]`, not the entire skill list. Empty array `[]` if no convention skills are relevant.
13. **relevant_req_ids (spec coverage)**: If `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/SPEC.compact.json` exists, set `relevant_req_ids` on every task. The IDs come from the `requirements[].id` field of the compact spec (format `R\d+`). Together, all tasks must collectively reference every requirement at least once. Cross-cutting requirements (encryption, audit logging, GDPR) may be referenced by multiple tasks. Use the `category` field on each requirement as a hint: `data-model` reqs belong to schema/migration tasks, `ui` reqs to component tasks, `auth` reqs to permission/middleware tasks, etc. An empty array means "no specific requirement, this is mechanical work" and should be rare.
input.new_string:
## Step 3: Create the Plan

Write `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/PLAN.json` with this exact format:

```json
{
  "title": "Feature-Titel",
  "summary": "Kurze Beschreibung was implementiert wird",
  "assumptions": ["Annahmen ueber die Codebase"],
  "out_of_scope": ["Was NICHT gemacht wird"],
  "phases": [
    {
      "id": "01",
      "name": "Phase-Name",
      "kind": "user-facing",
      "description": "Was in dieser Phase passiert",
      "merge_justification": "Ein Satz: warum diese Phase nicht mit der naechsten gemerged werden kann. Leer nur bei der letzten Phase.",
      "tasks": [
        {
          "id": "01-01",
          "name": "Task-Name",
          "description": "Detaillierte Beschreibung was zu tun ist",
          "files_to_create": ["neue/dateien.ext"],
          "files_to_modify": ["existierende/dateien.ext"],
          "depends_on": [],
          "model": "sonnet",
          "effort": "medium",
          "test_level": "L1",
          "convention_skills": ["ruby-code-writing", "form-builder"],
          "relevant_req_ids": ["R3", "R7"]
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "estimated_complexity": "medium",
  "risks": ["Potentielle Risiken"]
}
```

## Phase kind — how each phase relates to the user

Every phase declares a `kind` at plan time. Pick one:

| kind | when to pick | examples |
|---|---|---|
| `user-facing` | Phase delivers something a user clicks, types, or sees | "Admin user management UI", "Checkout flow", "Dark mode toggle" |
| `integration` | Phase delivers a subsystem another phase will expose to users | "Auth middleware + session", "tRPC router scaffold", "Background job runner" |
| `infrastructure` | Scaffolding, migrations, tooling, CI. No consumer within the run | "Scaffold Next.js + Prisma", "Set up CI" |

Downstream the contract validator enforces layer quotas based on this kind.
Pick wrong and the contract step will loudly reject the contract.

**Constraints enforced by the validator:**
- Max 1 `infrastructure` phase per plan. If you're thinking of two, merge them.
- Final phase must NOT be `infrastructure` — every run has to end on
  user-observable delivery.
- Two consecutive same-kind phases are a merge warning. If you keep them
  separate, the `merge_justification` must make a strong case.

## The merge-justification rule

Every phase except the last must have a `merge_justification` — one sentence
on why this phase cannot be merged with the next. The sentence is the forcing
function: if you can't write it, merge the phases.

Good justifications:
- "Exposes the auth API that phase 3's login UI consumes — splitting lets the
  auth contract probe the backend in isolation before UI lands on top."
- "Scaffolding Next.js and Prisma together produces a non-runnable
  intermediate state if split — kept as one infrastructure phase."
- "Delivers the admin user-management UI end-to-end; next phase introduces
  reporting which depends on this screen being stable."

Bad justifications (= merge these):
- "Separate concern." (vague)
- "Cleaner organization." (vague)
- "Makes each phase smaller." (self-fulfilling; re-merge them)

## Rules

1. **Max 4 phases, max 4 tasks per phase** — Pydantic and the validator both reject violations. If a feature genuinely needs more, split the SPEC into multiple dirigent runs rather than overflowing the plan.
2. **Every phase has a `kind`** — `user-facing`, `integration`, or `infrastructure`. Required. See the table above.
3. **Every phase except the last has a `merge_justification`** — one sentence, see the rule above.
4. **Max 1 infrastructure phase.** Scaffolds, migrations, and CI setup almost always belong together.
5. **Final phase MUST be `user-facing` or `integration`, not `infrastructure`.** A run that ends on infra delivered nothing observable.
6. Each task is atomic (does exactly one thing).
7. No dependencies between tasks within a phase.
8. Tasks must be concrete and executable.
9. If `BUSINESS_RULES.md` exists: all rules must be preserved.
10. **model**: "haiku" for simple tasks, "sonnet" for standard, "opus" for complex architecture.
11. **effort**: "low" for mechanical, "medium" for standard, "high" for complex logic.
12. **test_level**: "L1" for unit tests/lint, "L2" for integration tests, empty if no testing needed.
13. If `test-harness.json` exists: plan verification tasks that use its verification_commands and e2e_framework.run_command.
14. **Plan for maintainability** — task descriptions should guide toward scalable patterns: clear interfaces, separation of concerns, explicit dependencies.
15. **Plan for real verification** — each phase will be reviewed with executable commands. The reviewer will hit real endpoints and run real test suites.
16. **convention_skills**: If `.opencode/skills/` exists, tag each task with the skill names the coder should load. Empty array `[]` if no convention skills are relevant.
17. **relevant_req_ids (spec coverage)**: If `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/SPEC.compact.json` exists, set `relevant_req_ids` on every task. Together, all tasks must collectively reference every requirement at least once. Cross-cutting requirements (encryption, audit logging, GDPR) may be referenced by multiple tasks.

---
name: create-plan
description: Create a phased execution plan (PLAN.json) from spec and repo context
context: fork
agent: infra-architect
---

# Create Execution Plan

## Step 0: Resolve the Spec

Check for an existing spec in this order:

1. `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/SPEC.md` — written by the Dirigent CLI pipeline
2. `SPEC.md` in repo root
3. `.planning/SPEC.md`

**If a spec file exists:** read it and skip to Step 1.

**If no spec file exists:** generate one from user input.

### Generating a Spec

Check `$ARGUMENTS` for a feature description and for `--yolo`.

**Gather context first** — read these well-known files if they exist (regardless of mode):

- `ARCHITECTURE.md` — understand the system structure
- `README.md` — understand what the project does
- `package.json` or `pyproject.toml` — understand the tech stack
- `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/test-harness.json` — understand test infrastructure

**Normal mode** (no `--yolo`):

Using the feature description from `$ARGUMENTS` and the context you gathered, ask the user **at most 2-3 targeted questions** to fill in gaps. Focus only on things you genuinely cannot infer from the codebase:

- Scope boundaries (what's in vs out)
- Integration points that are ambiguous
- User-facing behavior that has multiple valid interpretations

Do NOT ask about:
- Tech stack (you can see it)
- File structure (you can see it)
- Testing approach (you can see it)
- Anything you can answer by reading the code

If the description is already clear and the codebase gives you enough context, ask zero questions.

**Yolo mode** (`--yolo` in arguments):

Ask zero questions. Use the feature description and codebase context to make reasonable assumptions. Document every assumption in the spec's `## Assumptions` section.

### Write the Spec

Write `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/SPEC.md`:

```markdown
# {Feature Title}

## Goal
{One paragraph: what this feature does and why}

## Requirements
{Bulleted list of concrete, testable requirements}

## Assumptions
{Bulleted list — especially important in --yolo mode}

## Out of Scope
{What this does NOT include}
```

Keep it short — 20-40 lines max. The spec exists to give the planner enough context, not to be a PRD.

---

## Step 1: Read Context

Read all available context files:

0. **Required if present:** `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/SPEC.compact.json` — pre-compacted requirements with stable IDs (R1, R2, …), glossary, entities, and flows. When this exists, you MUST tag every task in the plan with its `relevant_req_ids` (see Step 3 and Rule 13).
1. **Required:** `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/SPEC.md` — the feature specification (just written or pre-existing)
2. **Optional:** `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/BUSINESS_RULES.md` — business rules to preserve (Legacy route)
3. **Optional:** `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/CONTEXT.md` — relevant file analysis (Hybrid route)
4. **Optional:** `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/test-harness.json` — e2e test harness (base URL, auth, seed data, verification commands)
5. **Optional:** `ARCHITECTURE.md` — system architecture with XML-tagged sections. Key sections for planning:
   - `<testing-verification>` — build/test/e2e commands, test strategy. Tasks must follow this.
   - `<architecture-decisions>` — patterns, file organization, scaffold decisions. Tasks must follow these.
   - `<key-patterns>` — coding conventions and patterns. Task descriptions MUST reference relevant patterns.
6. **Optional:** `.opencode/skills/` — if this directory exists, list available skills by reading their SKILL.md frontmatter (the `name` and `description` fields). For each task, set the `convention_skills` array to skill names the coder should load. Match based on task content: Ruby files → `ruby-code-writing`, forms → `form-builder`, API endpoints → `api-v1-endpoints`, React → `react-components`, tests → `selenium-tests`, etc.
10. **Optional:** `.brv/context-tree/` — if this directory exists and `brv` CLI is available, run `brv query "What are the key domain patterns, rules, and architectural decisions for this project?"` to gather domain knowledge. Incorporate relevant findings into task descriptions so coder instances have domain context.

## Step 2: Analyze the Repo

Explore the repository structure relevant to the feature. Understand:
- Project language and framework
- Existing patterns and conventions
- File organization
- Available test infrastructure

## Step 3: Create the Plan

Write `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/PLAN.json` with this exact format:

```json
{
  "title": "Feature-Titel",
  "summary": "Kurze Beschreibung was implementiert wird",
  "assumptions": ["Annahmen ueber die Codebase"],
  "out_of_scope": ["Was NICHT gemacht wird"],
  "phases": [
    {
      "id": "01",
      "name": "Phase-Name",
      "description": "Was in dieser Phase passiert",
      "tasks": [
        {
          "id": "01-01",
          "name": "Task-Name",
          "description": "Detaillierte Beschreibung was zu tun ist",
          "files_to_create": ["neue/dateien.ext"],
          "files_to_modify": ["existierende/dateien.ext"],
          "depends_on": [],
          "model": "sonnet",
          "effort": "medium",
          "test_level": "L1",
          "convention_skills": ["ruby-code-writing", "form-builder"],
          "relevant_req_ids": ["R3", "R7"]
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "estimated_complexity": "medium",
  "risks": ["Potentielle Risiken"]
}
```

## Rules

1. **Max 4 phases, max 4 tasks per phase**
2. Each task is atomic (does exactly one thing)
3. No dependencies between tasks within a phase
4. Tasks must be concrete and executable
5. If `BUSINESS_RULES.md` exists: all rules must be preserved
6. **model**: "haiku" for simple tasks, "sonnet" for standard, "opus" for complex architecture
7. **effort**: "low" for mechanical, "medium" for standard, "high" for complex logic
8. **test_level**: "L1" for unit tests/lint, "L2" for integration tests, empty if no testing needed
9. If `test-harness.json` exists: plan verification tasks that use its verification_commands and e2e_framework.run_command. The reviewer will use these to verify features end-to-end.
10. **Plan for maintainability** — the agent executing these tasks is the long-term maintainer of the codebase. Task descriptions should guide toward scalable patterns: clear interfaces, separation of concerns, explicit dependencies. Do not plan throwaway code.
11. **Plan for real verification** — each phase will be reviewed with executable verification commands. Do not plan tasks that "work" only in the sense that they compile. The reviewer will hit real endpoints and run real test suites.
12. **convention_skills**: If `.opencode/skills/` exists, tag each task with the skill names the coder should load. Be specific — a task creating a Ruby form object needs `["ruby-code-writing", "form-builder"]`, not the entire skill list. Empty array `[]` if no convention skills are relevant.
13. **relevant_req_ids (spec coverage)**: If `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/SPEC.compact.json` exists, set `relevant_req_ids` on every task. The IDs come from the `requirements[].id` field of the compact spec (format `R\d+`). Together, all tasks must collectively reference every requirement at least once. Cross-cutting requirements (encryption, audit logging, GDPR) may be referenced by multiple tasks. Use the `category` field on each requirement as a hint: `data-model` reqs belong to schema/migration tasks, `ui` reqs to component tasks, `auth` reqs to permission/middleware tasks, etc. An empty array means "no specific requirement, this is mechanical work" and should be rare.

## Validation (MANDATORY)

After writing PLAN.json, validate it:

```bash
python ${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}/scripts/validate_schema.py ${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/PLAN.json
```

If validation fails, fix the errors and re-run until it passes.

input.old_string:
## Step 2: Classify the phase

Read the phase's name and description in `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/PLAN.json`. Pick one:

| Phase kind | When to pick | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| `user-facing` | The phase produces a UI surface or a user-observable behavior change. | "Admin can manage users", "Add dark mode toggle", "Checkout flow" |
| `integration` | The phase delivers a subsystem that a later phase will expose to users. Not user-visible yet, but has a clear downstream consumer. | "Auth middleware", "tRPC router scaffold", "Background job queue" |
| `infrastructure` | Scaffolding, migrations, tooling, CI. No consumer within the run. | "Scaffold Next.js app", "Prisma schema + initial migration", "GitHub Actions CI" |

When in doubt, prefer `user-facing`. Greenfield scaffolds often still have a
trivial user-visible surface (dev server serves default page) — proving that
end-to-end is higher-value than a bare `typecheck passes`.

Record your choice as `phase_kind` in the contract JSON. The validator
enforces layer quotas based on this field, and the final phase cannot be
classified `infrastructure`.
input.new_string:
## Step 2: Read the phase kind

The planner already classified the phase. Read `phase.kind` for this
phase from `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/PLAN.json` and copy it into the contract
as `phase_kind`. One of:

| Phase kind | Meaning | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| `user-facing` | Delivers a UI surface or user-observable behavior change. | "Admin can manage users", "Add dark mode toggle", "Checkout flow" |
| `integration` | Delivers a subsystem that a later phase will expose to users. | "Auth middleware", "tRPC router scaffold", "Background job queue" |
| `infrastructure` | Scaffolding, migrations, tooling, CI. No consumer within the run. | "Scaffold Next.js app", "Prisma schema + initial migration" |

**If the plan does not have `kind` on the phase** (legacy plan, missing field):
classify it yourself using the table above and record the choice in the
contract. Prefer `user-facing` in doubt — greenfield scaffolds often still
have a trivial user-visible surface (dev server serves default page) that
proves more than a bare `typecheck passes`.

**Do not contradict the planner.** If the plan says `user-facing` but there's
no user-observable work in the phase tasks, that's a planning bug — surface
it as a finding (a DEVIATION note in your output), do not silently reclassify.

The contract validator enforces layer quotas based on `phase_kind`, and
the final phase cannot be classified `infrastructure`.

Read the phase's name and description in `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/PLAN.json`. Pick one:

|---|---|---|
| `user-facing` | The phase produces a UI surface or a user-observable behavior change. | "Admin can manage users", "Add dark mode toggle", "Checkout flow" |
| `integration` | The phase delivers a subsystem that a later phase will expose to users. Not user-visible yet, but has a clear downstream consumer. | "Auth middleware", "tRPC router scaffold", "Background job queue" |
| `infrastructure` | Scaffolding, migrations, tooling, CI. No consumer within the run. | "Scaffold Next.js app", "Prisma schema + initial migration", "GitHub Actions CI" |

When in doubt, prefer `user-facing`. Greenfield scaffolds often still have a

end-to-end is higher-value than a bare `typecheck passes`.

**If the plan does not have `kind` on the phase** (legacy plan, missing field):

content.text:
Both tracks in, smoke tests all green. **5 files, +180/-39 lines.**

## What changed

**`plan_schema.py`** — `PhaseKind` moved here (phases are planned upstream of contracts). Added `Phase.kind` (required, defaults to `user-facing` for legacy plans via `Plan.load()`), `Phase.merge_justification` (default empty; validator enforces on non-final phases), `max_length=4` on `Plan.phases` and `Phase.tasks` — Pydantic now rejects overflow.

**`contract_schema.py`** — `PhaseKind` re-exported from plan_schema so existing imports keep working.

**`create-plan/scripts/validate_schema.py`** — phase-count cap, task-count cap, required `kind`, max 1 infrastructure phase, final phase ≠ infrastructure, required `merge_justification` on non-final phases, warning on consecutive same-kind phases.

**`create-plan/SKILL.md`** — phase-kind table, merge-justification rule with good/bad examples, rules renumbered to reflect enforcement.

**`create-contract/SKILL.md`** — Step 2 now says *read `phase.kind` from the plan* instead of re-classifying. If the plan's kind looks wrong, surface as DEVIATION rather than silently override.

## Smoke test results

| Case | Expected | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Well-formed 3-phase plan | PASS | ✅ |
| Final phase is `infrastructure` | FAIL with clear msg | ✅ |
| Two `infrastructure` phases | FAIL + merge warning | ✅ |
| Missing `merge_justification` on non-final | FAIL with forcing-function msg | ✅ |
| Inflated 5-phase plan (your 8-phase bug) | FAIL: "max is 4, split the SPEC" | ✅ |
| Legacy plan, no `kind`, no `merge_justification` | loads via backward-compat | ✅ |
| Contract flow post-move (PhaseKind identity) | same enum object | ✅ |

## One thing to flag

The `merge_justification` rule is the new forcing function. It's soft-enforceable — the validator checks that *a non-empty string exists*, not that the string is substantive. An LLM can still write `"merge_justification": "different concern"` and pass. We could add a heuristic (reject <50 chars, reject a blocklist of vague phrases) but that starts fighting LLMs on prose quality, which is a losing game. My lean: let the prose rule in the SKILL do the quality work, and if inflation persists we revisit.

Ready to commit?

file.content:
# Visitor Access Management (VAM) — Prototype Spec

*Ein Web-Prototyp, der den Kernmoment beweist: Credential scannen → Kontext sehen → Entscheidung treffen → Event loggen. Alles andere ist simuliert, weggelassen oder zu Text-Input vereinfacht.*

**Status:** Draft · **Scope:** 3–4 Wochen · **Demo-User:** 1 Institution

---

## 1 — Prototype-Philosophie

Ziel ist **nicht** ein Feature-vollständiges Produkt. Ziel ist der Beweis, dass der Datenfluss vom physischen Scan bis ins Dashboard funktioniert und der Membership-Koordinator zum ersten Mal sieht, was bisher unsichtbar war.

> **Leitsatz:** Tabellen und Kerninteraktion nah an Production. Hardware → Text-Input. Integrationen → CSV-Upload. Mobile → Web. Multi-User → Demo-User. Multi-Institution → eine Institution.

| Production-Realität | Prototype-Ersatz | |
|---|---|---|
| Kamera-Scanner (iOS/Android) | Text-Input: Token manuell eingeben oder aus Liste wählen | Simuliert |
| NFC-Tap / Wallet-Scan | Gleicher Text-Input | Simuliert |
| CRM-Sync (Blackbaud, Tessitura …) | CSV-Upload für Member-Import; kein Live-Sync | Simuliert |
| Multi-Station, Multi-Staff | Ein Demo-User, eine Station-ID hardcoded | Weggelassen |
| Post-Visit SMS (2–6h Delay) | Button „SMS senden" loggt Intent, zeigt Preview | Simuliert |
| Network Reporting (NARM/ASTC) | Nicht enthalten | Weggelassen |
| Pricing / Feature Flags | Nicht enthalten | Weggelassen |
| Offline-Mode | Nicht enthalten | Weggelassen |
| Datenmodell (Guests, Visits, Members) | **Production-nah** — echte Tabellen, echte Relationen | Production |
| Credential Resolution Engine | **Production-nah** — Token → Typ → Entitlement → Outcome | Production |
| Entry Dashboard | **Production-nah** — Live-Daten aus Visit-Tabelle | Production |

---

## 2 — Datenmodell (Production-Fundament)

Diese Tabellen sind das Fundament. Sie werden im Prototyp exakt so gebaut, wie sie später in Production stehen — Felder, Typen, Relationen. Was hier steht, wird nicht weggeworfen.

### members

Import via CSV. Repräsentiert den CRM-Stand der Institution.

```
members
├── id                    uuid        PK
├── institution_id        uuid        FK → institutions (hardcoded im Prototyp)
├── external_crm_id       string      ID aus Blackbaud/Tessitura/etc.
├── name                  string
├── email                 string
├── phone                 string      nullable
├── membership_tier       enum        basic | family | patron | vip
├── membership_status     enum        active | expired | lapsed
├── membership_expires_at date
├── guest_pass_total      int         pro Membership-Periode
├── guest_pass_used       int
├── reciprocal_networks   string[]    ["NARM", "ASTC"]
├── dm_token              string      unique — Barcode auf Digital Membership
└── imported_at           timestamp
```

### guests

Netto-neue Entität. Existiert heute nirgendwo in Cuseum.

```
guests
├── id                    uuid        PK
├── guest_token           string      unique — Barcode auf Guest Pass
├── referrer_member_id    uuid        FK → members
├── phone                 string      captured bei Wallet-Add
├── wallet_added_at       timestamp
├── institution_id        uuid
├── status                enum        active | consumed | expired
└── created_at            timestamp
```

### visits

Kern-Audit-Trail. Jeder Scan-Event wird hier geschrieben.

```
visits
├── id                    uuid        PK
├── token                 string      gescannter Barcode
├── credential_type       enum        dm_pass | guest_pass | day_ticket
├── member_id             uuid        nullable — FK → members
├── guest_id              uuid        nullable — FK → guests
├── outcome               enum        admit | deny | override
├── override_reason       string      nullable — Pflicht bei override
├── fraud_flags           string[]    ["duplicate_scan", "expired_override"]
├── station_id            string      hardcoded im Prototyp
├── staff_id              string      hardcoded im Prototyp
├── institution_id        uuid
├── scanned_at            timestamp
└── sms_triggered_at      timestamp   nullable
```

### fraud_rules (Konfigurationstabelle)

```
fraud_rules
├── id                    uuid
├── rule_type             enum        duplicate_scan | expired_override | guest_reuse | token_mismatch
├── window_minutes        int         z.B. 30 min für Duplicate Detection
├── severity              enum        info | warning | critical
└── active                boolean
```

---

## 3 — Die Kerninteraktion: Scan → Resolve → Decide → Log

Das ist der eine Moment, der den Wert beweist. Alles andere im Prototyp existiert, um diesen Flow zu ermöglichen oder sein Ergebnis sichtbar zu machen.

**01 — Token eingeben.** Der User tippt einen Barcode-String in ein Textfeld (simuliert den Kamera-Scan). Alternative: Dropdown mit vorhandenen Tokens aus der Demo-Datenbank.

**02 — Credential Resolution.** Das System identifiziert den Token-Typ (DM-Pass, Guest Pass, Day Ticket), schlägt die zugehörige Member- oder Guest-Entität nach und prüft Entitlement: Mitgliedschaft aktiv? Guest Pass noch nicht consumed? Duplicate Scan innerhalb des Zeitfensters?

**03 — Outcome-Anzeige mit Kontext.** Drei Zustände — grün / gelb / rot — jeweils mit spezifischem Kontext und empfohlener Aktion. Kein nacktes „Pass/Fail" mehr.

**04 — Admit / Override / Deny.** Ein Klick schreibt den Visit-Record. Bei Override: Pflicht-Dropdown für Reason. Bei Guest Pass: Status wechselt zu „consumed".

**05 — Fraud Detection.** Parallel zur Resolution prüft die Engine: Wurde dieser Token in den letzten X Minuten bereits gescannt? Wird ein expired Pass mit Override durchgewunken? Wird ein Guest Pass ein zweites Mal verwendet? Flags werden in `visits.fraud_flags` geschrieben.

**06 — Dashboard-Update.** Der neue Visit-Record erscheint sofort im Entry Dashboard — Live-Counter, Credential-Breakdown, Flags-Queue.

### Outcome-Zustände

**✓ ADMIT (grün)** — Membership aktiv, Guest Pass gültig, oder Day Ticket valid. Kontext: Name, Tier, verbleibende Guest Passes.

**⚠ REVIEW (gelb)** — Membership expired < 30 Tage, Guest Pass vom letzten Zyklus, Reciprocal-Claim ohne Match. Kontext: Ablaufdatum, Empfehlung, Renewal-Option.

**✕ DENY (rot)** — Membership lapsed > 30 Tage, Token unbekannt, Duplicate Scan, Guest Pass consumed. Kontext: Grund, letzte Aktivität, Override-Option.

---

## 4 — Screens

Der Prototyp hat drei Screens. Kein Login, kein Settings, kein Onboarding.

### Screen A — Scan Station (Hauptinteraktion)

Fullscreen-Fokus auf das Textfeld. Nach Token-Eingabe: sofortiger Wechsel zur Outcome-Anzeige mit Member/Guest-Kontext. Admit-Button prominent. Override-Flow als Secondary Action. Nach Admit: kurze Bestätigung, dann Reset für nächsten Scan.

Elemente: Token-Textfeld mit Autofocus, Outcome-Card (grün/gelb/rot) mit Kontext-Feldern (Name, Tier, Membership-Status, verbleibende Guest Passes, Ablaufdatum), Admit-Button, Override-Dropdown, letzte 5 Scans als Mini-Log unten.

### Screen B — Entry Dashboard

Perspektive des Membership-Koordinators. Sieht, was vorher unsichtbar war.

Elemente: Live Entry Count (heute), Guest Pass Count, Flags Count, Credential-Type-Breakdown (DM / Guest / Day Ticket), stündliche Traffic-Kurve für den aktuellen Tag, Flags-Queue (Duplicates, Overrides, Expired Admissions) mit Detail-Expand, Recent Entry Log mit Filter nach Credential Type und Outcome.

### Screen C — Data Setup

CSV-Upload für Member-Daten. Mapping-UI: User ordnet CSV-Spalten den Member-Feldern zu. Nach Import: Übersicht mit Anzahl importierter Records, Validierungsfehler. Zusätzlich: einfaches Formular zum manuellen Anlegen von Guest Passes (Token generieren, Referrer-Member auswählen).

---

## 5 — Credential Resolution Engine

Die Engine ist das Herz des Prototyps. Sie muss production-nah sein, weil die gesamte Scan-Logik auf ihr aufbaut.

```
// resolve(token)

1. Token Lookup
   → Suche in members.dm_token
   → Suche in guests.guest_token
   → Kein Match → return { outcome: DENY, reason: "unknown_token" }

2. Duplicate Check
   → SELECT FROM visits WHERE token = ? AND scanned_at > (now - window)
   → Match → flag "duplicate_scan", outcome abhängig von Severity-Config

3. Entitlement Check (je nach credential_type)
   DM Pass:
     → membership_status == active? → ADMIT
     → expired < 30d? → REVIEW + renewal_hint
     → lapsed > 30d? → DENY
   Guest Pass:
     → guest.status == active? → ADMIT + set consumed on admit
     → guest.status == consumed? → DENY + "already_used"
     → guest.status == expired? → DENY
   Day Ticket:
     → valid_date == today? → ADMIT
     → else → DENY

4. Context Assembly
   → Member: name, tier, expires_at, guest_passes_remaining
   → Guest: referrer_name, referrer_tier, wallet_added_at
   → Return: { outcome, credential_type, context, fraud_flags }
```

---

## 6 — Demo-Datensatz

Der Prototyp kommt mit vorinstallierten Demo-Daten, die alle Outcome-Pfade abdecken.

| Persona | Setup | Erwarteter Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Maria Chen — Patron | Active, Tier: Patron, 4/6 Guest Passes übrig | 🟢 Admit, zeigt Tier + Guest Pass Count |
| Tom Rivera — Basic | Expired vor 12 Tagen | 🟡 Review, zeigt Ablaufdatum + Renewal-Hint |
| Sarah Kim — Family | Lapsed vor 4 Monaten | 🔴 Deny |
| Guest von Maria | Active Guest Pass, Phone captured | 🟢 Admit, markiert als consumed |
| Guest von Maria (2. Scan) | Gleicher Token, bereits consumed | 🔴 Deny + Flag "guest_reuse" |
| Tom Rivera (2. Scan in 5 min) | Override beim 1. Mal, jetzt Duplicate | 🔴 Deny + Flag "duplicate_scan" |
| Unknown Token | Token existiert nicht | 🔴 Deny, "unknown_token" |

---

## 7 — Explizit nicht enthalten

Diese Entscheidungen sind bewusst. Jedes dieser Features wird erst gebaut, wenn der Kernflow validiert ist.

| Feature | Warum nicht |
|---|---|
| Native Mobile App | Hardware-Scan ist simuliert. Mobile kommt erst, wenn die Datenlogik steht. |
| CRM Live-Sync | Jede CRM-Integration ist institution-spezifisch. CSV-Import reicht zum Beweisen. |
| Post-Visit SMS | SMS-Versand ist ein Twilio-Call. Im Prototyp: Button zeigt Preview + loggt Intent. |
| GPR+ Attribution Funnel | Braucht echte Guest-Conversions über Wochen. Wird als statischer Report mit Demo-Daten angedeutet. |
| Network Reporting | Pro-Tier Feature. Nicht relevant für Kern-Validierung. |
| Multi-Institution | Eine Institution reicht. Mandantenfähigkeit ist Architektur, nicht Prototyp. |
| Offline Mode | Erfordert Service Worker + Sync Queue. Irrelevant für Demo-Umgebung. |
| Pricing / Feature Flags | Business Logic, nicht Produkt-Validierung. |

---

## 8 — Der Prototyp ist erfolgreich, wenn …

**01 — Scan → Outcome in <1 Sekunde.** Token eingeben, Ergebnis sehen. Die Latenz muss zeigen, dass Real-Time-Resolution machbar ist — auch wenn der „Scan" ein Textfeld ist.

**02 — Kontext am Point of Action.** Der Staff-User sieht bei jedem Scan: Wer ist diese Person? Welches Tier? Wann läuft ab? Wie viele Guest Passes? Das ist der Unterschied zu „grün oder rot".

**03 — Dashboard zeigt das Unsichtbare.** Der Koordinator öffnet das Dashboard und sieht zum ersten Mal: Wie viele Entries heute? Wie viele Guest Passes? Wie viele Overrides? Wie viele Flags? Die Antwort auf „Wie viel verlieren wir?" wird zum ersten Mal sichtbar.

**04 — Datenmodell überlebt den Prototyp.** Die Tabellen, Relationen und Enum-Werte werden ohne Breaking Changes in Production übernommen.

---

*VAM Prototype Spec — Cuseum × BID Equity · April 2026*

input.prompt:
Dogfood test for the updated dirigent plan-generation skill. We just changed the create-plan skill to enforce: max 4 phases, max 4 tasks per phase, required `phase.kind` (user-facing | integration | infrastructure), required `merge_justification` on every non-final phase, max 1 infrastructure phase, final phase cannot be infrastructure. Your job: run the skill for real on a real spec and see what you produce.

## Scenario

The SPEC is a greenfield web prototype — Visitor Access Management (VAM) for a cultural-institution SaaS. 3–4 week scope. Not a feature in an existing repo; treat the target as greenfield (no ARCHITECTURE.md, no BUSINESS_RULES.md, no .opencode/ conventions, no SPEC.compact.json, no test-harness.json).

## Paths

- SPEC.md: `/tmp/vam-test/SPEC.md` (already exists)
- Output PLAN.json to: `/tmp/vam-test/PLAN.json`
- Treat `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}` as `/tmp/vam-test` for the purposes of the skill
- Validator: `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/plugin/skills/create-plan/scripts/validate_schema.py`
- Run validator with: `cd /Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent && uv run python src/outbid_dirigent/plugin/skills/create-plan/scripts/validate_schema.py /tmp/vam-test/PLAN.json`

## What to do

1. Read the SPEC at `/tmp/vam-test/SPEC.md`. Understand the feature end-to-end — it has four tables, a credential resolution engine, three screens, demo seed data, and explicit "not in scope" items.
2. Read the latest create-plan skill at `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/plugin/skills/create-plan/SKILL.md` — the new rules live there.
3. Produce `/tmp/vam-test/PLAN.json` following the skill faithfully. Do NOT hand-wave the new fields: every phase must have a `kind`, every non-final phase must have a substantive `merge_justification` sentence.
4. Validate with the command above. If it fails, fix and re-run until it passes.
5. Report back in under 250 words:
   - The plan (phases + kinds + task counts) in a compact table
   - Validation output (PASS or FAIL + any warnings)
   - Honest observations: did the new rules help, hurt, or feel neutral on this spec? Did you feel constrained by max 4 phases × 4 tasks? Did merge_justification force better thinking or feel like a speed bump? Any rule that seemed wrong for this feature?

Be direct in the observations — you're dogfooding, not marketing the skill. If something sucked, say so.

content.text:
Passes. Expected warning for the consecutive user-facing phases. Here's the report:

---

## Plan Table

| Phase | Name | Kind | Tasks |
|-------|------|------|-------|
| 01 | Project Scaffold + Database Foundation | `infrastructure` | 4 |
| 02 | Credential Resolution Engine | `integration` | 4 |
| 03 | Scan Station — Screen A | `user-facing` | 4 |
| 04 | Entry Dashboard + Data Setup — Screens B + C | `user-facing` | 4 |

## Validation

```
WARNING: Two consecutive 'user-facing' phases — consider merging them unless merge_justification makes a strong case for separation.
VALIDATION PASSED
```

One warning, no errors. The warning is expected and documented in `risks[]`.

---

## Honest Observations

**Did max 4 phases × 4 tasks constrain meaningfully?** Yes, once. The 5-natural-chunk problem the advisor flagged is real: scaffold, engine, Screen A, Screen B, Screen C would be the clean split — but that's 5 phases. Merging B+C into phase 4 is defensible (both are coordinator-side, neither is transactional) but the resulting phase is genuinely dense — 04-02 (dashboard) and 04-03 (CSV import with column mapping) are each close to a "high" effort task on their own. If this were a real run, 04-03 is the task most likely to get the coder to cut corners or blow past the task boundary. The 4-cap forces a prioritization decision that belongs in the plan — good. But it also means an 8-task reality gets mapped to 4, which creates compression risk.

**Did merge_justification force better thinking?** Yes, specifically for phase 3→4. Writing it forced me to articulate the transactional/observational split, which is a real architectural reason to separate them in a contract validation step. Without the forcing function I would have described phases more loosely. The infra→engine and engine→scan justifications wrote themselves — those are natural layer boundaries. The scan→dashboard one required actual thought, which is the point.

**Did any rule feel wrong for this spec?** The consecutive `user-facing` warning is technically correct but weak on a 3-screen greenfield where two screens are observational and don't benefit from a phased API contract. The warning fires as noise here — you'd suppress it in the plan for the right reason, but it creates review work for a case where the separation is architecturally sound. A potential improvement: only warn on consecutive user-facing phases when neither phase's `merge_justification` references a concrete contract boundary. As-is, the rule fires unconditionally and puts the burden on the reviewer to determine if it's noise.

**The "max 1 infrastructure phase" rule:** felt completely natural here. Nothing ever wanted a second infra phase. Good constraint.

**Final phase cannot be infrastructure:** also natural. Never felt like a constraint on this spec.

{
  "title": "Visitor Access Management (VAM) — Greenfield Web Prototype",
  "summary": "Build a production-grade prototype proving the full Scan→Resolve→Decide→Log flow for cultural-institution visitor access management. Four tables (members, guests, visits, fraud_rules), a credential resolution engine, and three screens — Scan Station, Entry Dashboard, Data Setup.",
  "assumptions": [
    "Tech stack: Next.js (App Router) + TypeScript + Prisma + PostgreSQL — standard fullstack SaaS choice with no existing repo to constrain selection.",
    "Single demo institution; institution_id hardcoded as env var.",
    "Single demo user/station; no auth layer, no login screen.",
    "Day Ticket support is minimal: single valid_date field, no separate day_tickets table needed for prototype.",
    "Fraud window config (duplicate_scan window) defaults to 30 minutes, editable via seed data.",
    "CSV import runs server-side; no streaming required for prototype-scale data.",
    "SMS intent (button logs to visits.sms_triggered_at) is a stub — no Twilio call.",
    "No real-time WebSocket for dashboard; polling or manual refresh is acceptable for prototype."
  ],
  "out_of_scope": [
    "Native mobile app / camera scanner",
    "CRM live sync (Blackbaud, Tessitura)",
    "Post-visit SMS delivery via Twilio",
    "Network reporting (NARM/ASTC)",
    "Multi-institution tenancy",
    "Offline mode / service worker",
    "Pricing and feature flags",
    "Authentication / login screen",
    "GPR+ attribution funnel"
  ],
  "phases": [
    {
      "id": "01",
      "name": "Project Scaffold + Database Foundation",
      "kind": "infrastructure",
      "description": "Initialize the Next.js + TypeScript + Prisma + PostgreSQL project. Define all four production-grade tables (members, guests, visits, fraud_rules) as Prisma schema with correct enums, relations, and indexes. Run initial migration. Seed the demo dataset covering all seven outcome personas from the spec. Configure environment and project tooling.",
      "merge_justification": "The scaffold and schema must be committed and migrated before any resolution engine code can be written or tested — splitting this from phase 2 lets the database contract (table shapes, enum values, FK relations) be verified in isolation before domain logic is layered on top.",
      "tasks": [
        {
          "id": "01-01",
          "name": "Initialize Next.js project with TypeScript, Prisma, and PostgreSQL",
          "description": "Bootstrap a Next.js 14 App Router project with TypeScript strict mode. Install and configure Prisma with a PostgreSQL datasource. Set up .env.local with DATABASE_URL. Configure ESLint + Prettier. Establish the src/ directory structure: app/, lib/, components/, prisma/. Verify `prisma generate` and `next build` run clean.",
          "files_to_create": [
            "package.json",
            "tsconfig.json",
            "next.config.ts",
            ".env.local",
            ".env.example",
            "prisma/schema.prisma",
            "src/app/layout.tsx",
            "src/app/page.tsx"
          ],
          "files_to_modify": [],
          "depends_on": [],
          "model": "sonnet",
          "effort": "medium",
          "test_level": "L1",
          "convention_skills": [],
          "relevant_req_ids": []
        },
        {
          "id": "01-02",
          "name": "Define Prisma schema for all four tables and run migration",
          "description": "Write the complete Prisma schema for members (dm_token unique, enums: MembershipTier, MembershipStatus), guests (guest_token unique, status enum: active|consumed|expired, FK to members), visits (FK to members and guests nullable, outcome enum: admit|deny|override, fraud_flags String[], credential_type enum: dm_pass|guest_pass|day_ticket), fraud_rules (rule_type enum, severity enum, window_minutes, active). Add indexes on members.dm_token, guests.guest_token, visits.token+scanned_at (for duplicate detection window query). Run `prisma migrate dev --name init`.",
          "files_to_create": [
            "prisma/migrations/0001_init/"
          ],
          "files_to_modify": [
            "prisma/schema.prisma"
          ],
          "depends_on": [],
          "model": "sonnet",
          "effort": "medium",
          "test_level": "L1",
          "convention_skills": [],
          "relevant_req_ids": []
        },
        {
          "id": "01-03",
          "name": "Create demo seed script covering all seven outcome personas",
          "description": "Write prisma/seed.ts that inserts: one institution (hardcoded UUID as INSTITUTION_ID env), four members (Maria Chen Patron active 4/6 guest passes, Tom Rivera Basic expired 12 days ago, Sarah Kim Family lapsed 4 months ago, one member with reciprocal network claim), guest passes (one active for Maria, one already-consumed for reuse test), fraud_rules rows (duplicate_scan window 30min critical, guest_reuse warning, expired_override info). Ensure all seven spec personas produce their expected outcomes. Configure `prisma.config.ts` seed command.",
          "files_to_create": [
            "prisma/seed.ts",
            "prisma/config.ts"
          ],
          "files_to_modify": [
            "package.json"
          ],
          "depends_on": [],
          "model": "sonnet",
          "effort": "medium",
          "test_level": "L1",
          "convention_skills": [],
          "relevant_req_ids": []
        },
        {
          "id": "01-04",
          "name": "Create Prisma client singleton and shared TypeScript types",
          "description": "Create src/lib/prisma.ts exporting a singleton PrismaClient instance (hot-reload safe for Next.js dev). Export typed interfaces derived from Prisma-generated types: ResolvedVisit (visit + member + guest joined), OutcomeState ('admit'|'review'|'deny'), ResolutionContext (name, tier, expires_at, guest_passes_remaining, referrer info). These types are the contract between the resolution engine (phase 2) and the UI (phase 3).",
          "files_to_create": [
            "src/lib/prisma.ts",
            "src/types/vam.ts"
          ],
          "files_to_modify": [],
          "depends_on": [],
          "model": "sonnet",
          "effort": "low",
          "test_level": "L1",
          "convention_skills": [],
          "relevant_req_ids": []
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "02",
      "name": "Credential Resolution Engine",
      "kind": "integration",
      "description": "Implement the pure resolution logic as a server-side service: token lookup (members.dm_token → members, guests.guest_token → guests), duplicate scan detection (windowed query on visits), entitlement checks per credential type (DM Pass active/expired/lapsed thresholds, Guest Pass status machine, Day Ticket date match), fraud flag assembly, and context assembly. Expose as a Next.js Route Handler (POST /api/resolve) that returns OutcomeState + context. No UI — testable with curl or Playwright API calls against the seeded data.",
      "merge_justification": "The resolution engine is a pure integration layer — no UI, no user interaction — and its correctness can and should be verified via direct API probes against the seven demo personas before any UI is built on top. Keeping it separate lets phase-3 contract validation confirm all outcome paths are correct before the scan station UI introduces visual complexity.",
      "tasks": [
        {
          "id": "02-01",
          "name": "Implement token lookup and credential type detection",
          "description": "Write src/lib/engine/lookup.ts: given a token string, query members WHERE dm_token = token (returns DM Pass) and guests WHERE guest_token = token (returns Guest Pass). Unknown token returns { outcome: 'deny', reason: 'unknown_token' }. Day ticket detection: if token matches format 'DAY-YYYYMMDD', parse date directly (no table lookup needed for prototype). Return typed LookupResult discriminated union.",
          "files_to_create": [
            "src/lib/engine/lookup.ts"
          ],
          "files_to_modify": [],
          "depends_on": [],
          "model": "sonnet",
          "effort": "medium",
          "test_level": "L1",
          "convention_skills": [],
          "relevant_req_ids": []
        },
        {
          "id": "02-02",
          "name": "Implement duplicate detection and fraud flag engine",
          "description": "Write src/lib/engine/fraud.ts: query visits WHERE token = ? AND scanned_at > (now - window_minutes). Read active fraud_rules from DB (cached per request). For each active rule type, evaluate and accumulate fraud flags string[]. Return { flags: string[], hasCriticalFlag: boolean }. Critical flags (duplicate_scan) affect outcome; warning flags (expired_override, guest_reuse) are logged but don't hard-deny.",
          "files_to_create": [
            "src/lib/engine/fraud.ts"
          ],
          "files_to_modify": [],
          "depends_on": [],
          "model": "sonnet",
          "effort": "medium",
          "test_level": "L1",
          "convention_skills": [],
          "relevant_req_ids": []
        },
        {
          "id": "02-03",
          "name": "Implement entitlement checks and context assembly",
          "description": "Write src/lib/engine/entitlement.ts: DM Pass check (active→ADMIT, expired<30d→REVIEW+renewal_hint, lapsed>30d→DENY), Guest Pass check (active→ADMIT, consumed→DENY+already_used, expired→DENY), Day Ticket check (valid_date==today→ADMIT, else→DENY). Write src/lib/engine/context.ts: assemble ResolutionContext from member/guest data (name, tier, expires_at, guest_passes_remaining = total - used, referrer info for guest passes). Export resolve(token) as the single entry point composing all four modules.",
          "files_to_create": [
            "src/lib/engine/entitlement.ts",
            "src/lib/engine/context.ts",
            "src/lib/engine/index.ts"
          ],
          "files_to_modify": [],

          "effort": "high",
          "test_level": "L1",
          "convention_skills": [],
          "relevant_req_ids": []
        },

          "description": "Write src/app/api/resolve/route.ts: accepts { token: string }, calls resolve(token), returns ResolutionResult JSON. Write src/app/api/admit/route.ts: accepts { token, outcome: 'admit'|'override'|'deny', override_reason?: string }, writes the visits row (station_id and staff_id from env), mutates guest.status to 'consumed' when outcome is admit for a guest pass, returns the created visit ID. Both handlers must respond in <200ms on seeded data (no N+1 queries). Verify all seven seed personas return expected outcomes via curl against local dev server.",
          "files_to_create": [
            "src/app/api/resolve/route.ts",
            "src/app/api/admit/route.ts"
          ],
          "files_to_modify": [],
          "depends_on": [],
          "model": "sonnet",
          "effort": "medium",
          "test_level": "L2",
          "convention_skills": [],
          "relevant_req_ids": []
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "03",
      "name": "Scan Station — Screen A",
      "kind": "user-facing",
      "description": "Build the fullscreen transactional scan interface. Token text input with autofocus, immediate API call to /api/resolve on submit, outcome card rendering three states (green/yellow/red) with member/guest context fields, Admit / Override / Deny action buttons, override reason dropdown (required on override), mini scan log of last 5 entries. After admit: brief confirmation then auto-reset for next scan. This is the core demo moment — all interaction must feel instantaneous (<1s from token entry to outcome display).",
      "merge_justification": "Screen A is the transactional write path (every admit/override/deny creates a visits row) and is the primary demo deliverable. Screen B reads from the visits table Screen A populates, and Screen C (CSV upload) loads the member data the resolution engine queries. On submit: POST to /api/resolve, show loading state, render OutcomeCard. Handle network errors with a red alert. Reset button clears the form and returns to input state.",

lastPrompt: can you try that plan generation skill directly on the @example-specs/cuseum-vam-small.md

content.text:
Plan passes validation. Substantive agent feedback. Here's the takeaway.

## What the planner produced

| Phase | Kind | Tasks | Effort mix |
|---|---|---|---|
| 01 — Scaffold + DB | `infrastructure` | 4 | low/medium |
| 02 — Resolution Engine | `integration` | 4 | medium/high (1 opus) |
| 03 — Scan Station | `user-facing` | 4 | low/medium |
| 04 — Dashboard + Data Setup | `user-facing` | 4 | medium/high (2 high) |

Validator: **VALIDATION PASSED** + one warning ("two consecutive user-facing phases").

## What worked

- **`merge_justification` paid off.** The phase 03→04 boundary sentence — *"Screen A is the transactional write path... Screen B reads from the visits table Screen A populates... separating allows Screen A to be contract-validated as a complete interaction loop before the observability layer is added"* — is real architectural reasoning. Without the forcing function the planner admits it would've been looser prose.
- **`max 1 infrastructure` and `final ≠ infrastructure` felt invisible.** Never wanted a second infra phase. Never wanted to end on one.
- **The `phase_kind` classification mapped cleanly** to the spec's natural shape (scaffold / engine / scan / dashboard-and-setup).

## What hurt

- **4×4 = 16 task cap is tight on this spec.** The natural split is 5 chunks (scaffold / engine / scan / dashboard / data-setup); merging dashboard + data-setup into phase 04 created a dense phase with two `effort: "high"` tasks (dashboard and CSV mapping UI). Planner flagged this explicitly in `risks[]`: *"CSV column mapping UI is complex enough to consume most of phase 4 capacity — if it slips, the guest pass form (04-04) is the natural cut."* That's the validator cost showing up in risk management, which is actually fine — but worth knowing.

- **The consecutive-same-kind warning is noisy on legitimately distinct user-facing phases.** The agent's critique: *"The warning fires unconditionally and puts the burden on the reviewer to determine if it's noise."* For user-facing specifically, different screens often serve different users (scan-station staff vs. dashboard coordinator) — consecutive user-facing is frequently correct.

## Two decisions surfaced

1. **Should the consecutive-same-kind warning skip `user-facing` pairs?** I'd lean yes — user-facing phases split along user-journey boundaries by nature; the noise-to-signal ratio is low there. Keep the warning for consecutive `integration` (real over-slicing) and wouldn't fire for `infrastructure` at all since max is already 1. Small validator tweak, 3 lines.

2. **Is 16 total tasks the right ceiling?** For this spec it barely fit. For something simpler (your 8-phase API change counter-example) it's generous. The cap is blunt: a feature that genuinely needs 18 atomic tasks gets compressed. Options: leave as-is and trust "split SPEC into multiple runs," or add a single per-run override (`plan.complexity_override: "large"` raises to 5×5). I lean leave-as-is unless you see this hurt in practice — scope discipline beats flexibility here.

Push back on either, or want me to apply #1 and commit what's done?

message.content: 1 is a no-breainer - do it. 2: the 5*5 increase in exemptions is flexible enough while not too flexible at the same time.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
"""
Plan schema — typed representation of PLAN.json.

Uses pydantic for validation, serialization, and deserialization.
"""

import json
import logging
from enum import Enum
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Optional

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


class PhaseKind(str, Enum):
    """How a phase relates to the user.

    Drives layer quotas in contracts and plan-level validation (max 1
    infrastructure, final phase ≠ infrastructure, etc).
    """
    USER_FACING = "user-facing"        # Delivers UI surface or observable behavior change
    INTEGRATION = "integration"        # Subsystem a later phase will expose to users
    INFRASTRUCTURE = "infrastructure"  # Scaffolding, migrations, tooling — no consumer in-run


class Task(BaseModel):
    """A single executable task within a phase."""
    id: str
    name: str
    description: str = ""
    files_to_create: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    files_to_modify: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    depends_on: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    model: str = ""
    effort: str = ""
    test_level: str = ""  # "", "L1", "L2"
    convention_skills: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)  # e.g. ["ruby-code-writing", "form-builder"]
    relevant_req_ids: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)  # e.g. ["R3", "R7"] from SPEC.compact.json


class Phase(BaseModel):
    """A group of related tasks executed sequentially."""
    id: str
    name: str
    kind: PhaseKind = Field(
        default=PhaseKind.USER_FACING,
        description="user-facing | integration | infrastructure — drives contract layer quotas",
    )
    description: str = ""
    merge_justification: str = Field(
        default="",
        description="One sentence: why this phase can't be merged with the next. Empty only if this is the last phase.",
    )
    tasks: list[Task] = Field(default_factory=list, max_length=4)

    def model_post_init(self, __context):
        # Normalize: accept both "id" and "phase" field names
        if not self.id and hasattr(self, "phase"):
            self.id = str(getattr(self, "phase"))


class Plan(BaseModel):
    """The full execution plan for a dirigent run."""
    title: str = "Untitled"
    summary: str = ""
    phases: list[Phase] = Field(default_factory=list, max_length=4)
    estimated_complexity: str = "medium"
    risks: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    assumptions: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    out_of_scope: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)

    @property
    def total_tasks(self) -> int:
        return sum(len(p.tasks) for p in self.phases)

    @property
    def all_tasks(self) -> list[tuple[Task, Phase]]:
        """Flat list of (task, phase) in execution order."""
        return [(t, p) for p in self.phases for t in p.tasks]

    def task_position(self, task_id: str) -> Optional[dict]:
        """Position info for a task: index, total, phase, prev/next."""
        tasks = self.all_tasks
        idx = next((i for i, (t, _) in enumerate(tasks) if t.id == task_id), None)
        if idx is None:
            return None

        t, p = tasks[idx]
        info = {
            "index": idx + 1,
            "total": len(tasks),
            "phase_id": p.id,
            "phase_name": p.name,
            "total_phases": len(self.phases),
        }
        if idx > 0:
            prev_t, _ = tasks[idx - 1]
            info["prev_id"] = prev_t.id
            info["prev_name"] = prev_t.name
        if idx < len(tasks) - 1:
            next_t, _ = tasks[idx + 1]
            info["next_id"] = next_t.id
            info["next_name"] = next_t.name
        return info

    def save(self, path: Path):
        path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        path.write_text(
            self.model_dump_json(indent=2, exclude_none=True),
            encoding="utf-8",
        )

    @classmethod
    def load(cls, path: Path) -> Optional["Plan"]:
        """Load from PLAN.json. Returns None if missing or invalid."""
        if not path.exists():
            return None
        try:
            raw = json.loads(path.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
            # Normalize phase/task fields from common LLM output variations
            for p in raw.get("phases", []):
                if "phase" in p and "id" not in p:
                    p["id"] = str(p.pop("phase"))
                elif "id" in p:
                    p["id"] = str(p["id"])
                # Pre-plan-tuning plans have no phase kind — default to user-facing
                # (tightest downstream contract quotas; loudest if misclassified)
                if "kind" not in p:
                    p["kind"] = "user-facing"
                for t in p.get("tasks", []):
                    # title → name
                    if "title" in t and "name" not in t:
                        t["name"] = t.pop("title")
                    # files → files_to_modify (conservative default)
                    if "files" in t and "files_to_modify" not in t and "files_to_create" not in t:
                        t["files_to_modify"] = t.pop("files")
            return cls.model_validate(raw)
        except json.JSONDecodeError as e:
            logger.error("PLAN.json is not valid JSON: %s", e)
            return None
        except Exception as e:
            logger.error("PLAN.json validation failed: %s", e)
            return None

    @staticmethod
    def json_template() -> str:
        """JSON template string for the plan creation prompt."""
        return """{
  "title": "Feature-Titel",
  "summary": "Kurze Beschreibung was implementiert wird",
  "assumptions": [
    "Annahmen die du über die Codebase/das Feature machst",
    "z.B. 'Tests laufen mit pytest', 'API ist REST-basiert'"
  ],
  "out_of_scope": [
    "Was NICHT Teil dieses Plans ist",
    "z.B. 'Deployment/CI-Pipeline', 'Performance-Optimierung'"
  ],
  "phases": [
    {
      "id": "01",
      "name": "Phase-Name",
      "kind": "user-facing|integration|infrastructure",
      "description": "Was in dieser Phase passiert",
      "merge_justification": "Ein Satz: warum diese Phase nicht mit der naechsten gemerged werden kann. Leer nur bei der letzten Phase.",
      "tasks": [
        {
          "id": "01-01",
          "name": "Task-Name",
          "description": "Detaillierte Beschreibung was zu tun ist",
          "files_to_create": ["liste/von/neuen/dateien.ext"],
          "files_to_modify": ["liste/von/existierenden/dateien.ext"],
          "depends_on": [],
          "model": "sonnet|haiku|opus (welches Modell für diesen Task am besten ist)",
          "effort": "low|medium|high (wie viel Denkaufwand nötig ist)",
          "test_level": "L1|L2| (welches Test-Level nach diesem Task laufen soll, leer wenn kein Test nötig)",
          "convention_skills": ["skill-name-1", "skill-name-2 (aus .opencode/skills/, leer wenn keine)"],
          "relevant_req_ids": ["R3", "R7 (IDs aus SPEC.compact.json — welche Requirements dieser Task adressiert)"]
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "estimated_complexity": "low|medium|high",
  "risks": ["Liste von potentiellen Risiken"]
}"""
